case_name: Airlines for America v. U.S. Department of Transportation
court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
eligibility:
This is a tracking-only listing. There is NO party status or individual complaint mechanism in THIS specific case.

You may want to follow this case if:
- You travel with a wheelchair, scooter, or other mobility aid by air.
- You've had wheelchair damage or other ACAA-protected issues with an airline.
- You're a disability-rights advocate, policy professional, or stakeholder following federal agency rulemaking.
- You're a member of a disability-rights organization that may file amicus briefs (PVA, NCIL, NFB, ACB, NDRN).

For active reporting of airline wheelchair damage incidents, see the airline-wheelchair-damage listing.
eligibility_professional: Tracking-only listing. No individual party-status, intervention, or complaint mechanism is available within this APA challenge. Existing ACAA-protected rights of passengers remain unaffected during the pendency of the litigation. Direct individual remedies for current incidents remain available under the existing Part 382 framework via DOT administrative complaint (see airline-wheelchair-damage).
eligibility_simple:
There's nothing for you to file in THIS case. It's a court case between the airline industry and DOT.

You might want to know about it if:
- You travel by air with a wheelchair or other mobility aid.
- You've had a wheelchair damaged on a flight.
- You're in a disability rights group.

For an actual wheelchair-damage problem, file with DOT at airconsumer.dot.gov (see airline-wheelchair-damage).
full_description:
Airlines for America (A4A), the major US airline industry trade association, sued the U.S. Department of Transportation in February 2025 to block DOT's strengthened wheelchair-handling rule. The case is pending in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit.

What's at stake: in 2024, DOT finalized a rule strengthening 14 C.F.R. Part 382 — the regulations that govern how airlines handle wheelchairs and other mobility aids. The rule added enforcement teeth: higher civil penalties for damage and loss, clearer operational standards, better complaint mechanisms. A4A argues that DOT exceeded its authority and that the rule is arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act. If A4A wins (in whole or in part), some of the new protections could be vacated or sent back to DOT. If DOT wins, the strengthened rule stays in force.

What this means for you: this case is tracking-only — there's no individual complaint to file as part of THIS case. Existing wheelchair-handling rights under the ACAA and current Part 382 regulations remain in force during the litigation. Damaged-wheelchair incidents should still be reported via DOT (airconsumer.dot.gov) — see the airline-wheelchair-damage listing. Organizations like Paralyzed Veterans of America and the National Council on Independent Living welcome member engagement.

This listing exists because Ada users dealing with airline wheelchair issues should know the legal framework is currently under attack — but their existing rights are still in effect.
full_description_professional:
Airlines for America v. U.S. Department of Transportation, pending in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit (filed Feb. 14, 2025), is an industry petition for review of DOT's 2024 final rule amending 14 C.F.R. Part 382 (the Air Carrier Access Act implementing regulations). A4A advances Administrative Procedure Act challenges including arguments that: (i) DOT exceeded its statutory authority under 49 U.S.C. § 41705; (ii) the rule is arbitrary and capricious in its cost-benefit analysis; (iii) certain provisions exceed the scope of the underlying statutory authorization.

The 2024 final rule strengthened: (i) civil-penalty exposure for repeat or egregious mishandling; (ii) operational standards including pre-flight notification, mobility-aid storage protocols, and incident-handling procedures; (iii) consumer-complaint mechanisms including timeline requirements for carrier responses.

The Fifth Circuit has issued several recent decisions narrowing agency rulemaking authority (e.g., post-Loper Bright deference jurisprudence). Outcome handicap is uncertain; possible dispositions range from full upholding through partial vacatur and remand. Disability-community amicus participation includes (or is likely to include) PVA, NCIL, NDRN, NFB, ACB, and similar organizations.

Pending litigation does not stay the rule's effectiveness absent a separate stay order. ACAA-protected travelers retain full existing rights; current DOT enforcement continues. This listing is tracking-only — no individual party-status or complaint mechanism is available within this regulatory-challenge proceeding.

For passengers experiencing current wheelchair damage, see airline-wheelchair-damage. For amicus support and policy engagement, contact PVA (pva.org), NCIL (ncil.org), and similar advocacy organizations.
full_description_simple:
The airline industry is suing the U.S. Department of Transportation. They want to weaken the rules that protect wheelchairs and other mobility aids on airplanes.

In 2024 DOT made the rules stronger:
- Bigger fines for airlines that break wheelchairs.
- Clearer rules about how to handle a wheelchair.
- Better ways for passengers to complain.

The airlines (a group called Airlines for America, or A4A) say DOT went too far. They sued. The case is in a federal appeals court called the Fifth Circuit.

If the airlines win, some of the new protections could be undone.
If DOT wins, the new rules stay.

What this means for you:
- This case is just something to watch. You can't file your own complaint in THIS case.
- The current wheelchair rules are STILL IN EFFECT during the case. So your rights still apply.
- If your wheelchair gets damaged on a flight, file a DOT complaint (see related airline-wheelchair-damage listing).
- If you want to help the disability side, organizations like Paralyzed Veterans of America (PVA) accept members and file briefs in these cases.

key_dates.Court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
key_dates.Status: Pending; oral argument not yet scheduled or briefing schedule pending

legal_theory: APA challenge to DOT Wheelchair Rule (14 CFR Part 382 amendments)
short_description: Airline industry sued to block DOT's strengthened wheelchair-handling rules. If A4A wins, protections weaken. Tracking only.
short_description_professional: Airlines for America v. U.S. DOT, pending in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit (filed Feb. 14, 2025). Industry APA challenge to DOT's strengthened 14 C.F.R. Part 382 wheelchair-and-mobility-aid handling rule. If A4A prevails, the 2024 enhanced enforcement framework — including new civil-penalty exposure and operational standards — could be vacated or remanded. Tracking-only listing.
short_description_simple: The airline industry is in court trying to weaken the rules that protect wheelchairs and mobility devices on flights. If they win, broken-wheelchair protections could get weaker. This is something to watch — not something you can directly file a complaint about.

case_name: Texas v. Kennedy (formerly Texas v. Becerra)
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Texas
defendants: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
eligibility:
This is a tracking-only listing. There is NO party status or individual complaint mechanism in THIS specific case.

You may want to follow this case if:
- You or a family member is in a nursing facility or other institutional placement and wants community-based services.
- You have experienced medical-equipment accessibility issues.
- You've had healthcare decisions affected by disability-related value assessments.
- You're a disability-rights advocate, policy professional, or stakeholder.
- You're a member of a disability-rights organization that may file amicus briefs (NDRN, AAPD, Bazelon, DREDF, state P&A).

For active individual situations, see the related cross-referenced listings — the existing rule and underlying Section 504 framework still provide enforcement pathways.
eligibility_professional: Tracking-only listing. No individual party-status, intervention, or complaint mechanism is available within this APA + constitutional challenge proceeding. Existing § 504 protections under the predecessor regulations remain enforceable; the underlying statutory framework (29 U.S.C. § 794) is unaffected by the APA challenge to the implementing regulation. Affected individuals seeking direct enforcement should pursue HHS OCR § 504 complaints under predecessor frameworks while the rule's status is litigated.
eligibility_simple:
There's nothing for you to file in THIS case. It's a court case between states and the federal government.

You might want to know about it if:
- You or a family member is in a nursing home and wants to come home (see Idaho investigation).
- You have medical equipment access issues (see medical-exam-table-access).
- You're in a disability rights group.

For actual current problems, see the related listings.
full_description:
Multiple state attorneys general — led by Texas — are suing the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to challenge HHS's 2024 final rule implementing Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. The case is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas.

The case name changed during the litigation as HHS leadership changed: originally Texas v. Becerra, subsequently restyled Texas v. Kennedy (Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is the current HHS Secretary as of 2025).

What's at stake: HHS's 2024 Section 504 rule (89 Fed. Reg. 40066, May 9, 2024) is the first comprehensive update to the Section 504 regulations in decades. It codifies and strengthens many protections, including the Olmstead integration mandate (the principle that people with disabilities have the right to live in the most integrated setting appropriate — not forced into institutions). The rule also strengthens accessibility requirements for medical equipment, online accessibility, value assessments in healthcare decisions, and child welfare disability discrimination. The state plaintiffs argue HHS exceeded its authority and the rule is arbitrary and capricious.

What this could mean: if state plaintiffs win (in whole or in part), some of the rule's protections could be vacated. The integration mandate in particular is a major target — it's the foundation of cases like the DOJ investigation of Idaho's nursing-facility integration practices. If HHS wins, the rule stays in force.

This case is tracking-only — there's no individual complaint to file. Existing § 504 rights remain in force during the litigation. Organizations like NDRN, AAPD, the Bazelon Center, and state P&A entities are active in the case.
full_description_professional:
Texas v. Kennedy (formerly Texas v. Becerra), pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas, is a multi-state Administrative Procedure Act and constitutional challenge to HHS's 2024 final rule under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act (89 Fed. Reg. 40066, May 9, 2024). The case caption updated from "Becerra" to "Kennedy" reflects the post-2024 change in HHS Secretary.

The 2024 final rule represents the first comprehensive update to the Section 504 regulations (45 C.F.R. Part 84) in decades. Substantive provisions challenged include:
- Codification of Olmstead integration-mandate principles (45 C.F.R. § 84.76), grounded in Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999), and 28 C.F.R. § 35.130(d).
- Medical-diagnostic-equipment accessibility requirements paralleling the DOJ MDE Rule.
- Online accessibility standards.
- Value-assessment prohibitions in healthcare decision-making (addressing Quality-Adjusted Life Year and similar metrics).
- Child welfare and parental-disability protections.
- Specific provisions for substance-use disorder, HIV, and chronic-condition protections.

State plaintiffs argue HHS exceeded statutory authority under 29 U.S.C. § 794, advanced arbitrary-and-capricious rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A), and (in some pleadings) raised constitutional challenges. Particular targets include the integration mandate and the value-assessment provisions.

The case is pending; preliminary injunction motions and stays have been litigated. Disability-community amicus participation includes NDRN, AAPD, Bazelon Center for Mental Health Law, the National Disability Rights Network state P&A entities, the Disability Rights Education and Defense Fund (DREDF), and others.

The 2024 rule remains in effect absent a stay. Existing § 504 protections under predecessor regulations remain in force. Tracking-only listing — no individual party-status or complaint mechanism within this proceeding. For affected individual cases: see doj-investigation-idaho-nursing (Olmstead), medical-exam-table-access (MDE), and other relevant listings.
full_description_simple:
A bunch of state attorneys general are suing the federal government. They want to weaken a rule that protects the rights of people with disabilities.

The rule:
- In 2024, the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) updated the rules for Section 504. Section 504 is a key federal disability-rights law from 1973. The update was the biggest in decades.
- It strengthens the right to live in your own home or community — not in a nursing home. (This is called the "Olmstead" principle, named after a 1999 Supreme Court case.)
- It also strengthens rules about medical equipment accessibility, online accessibility, child welfare, and more.

The lawsuit:
- States led by Texas sued HHS. The case was originally called Texas v. Becerra. It's now called Texas v. Kennedy because HHS has a new Secretary.
- The states say HHS went too far and that the rule should be thrown out.

What this could mean:
- If the states win, parts of the rule could be undone.
- The community-integration part is a big target. This is the rule that backs cases like the DOJ investigation into Idaho's use of nursing homes.
- If HHS wins, the rule stays.

What this means for you:
- This case is just something to watch. There's no complaint to file here.
- Your current Section 504 rights are still active during the case.
- If you're dealing with a nursing-home situation, see the Idaho investigation listing.
- If you're dealing with medical equipment access, see medical-exam-table-access.
- Disability rights organizations like NDRN, AAPD, and the Bazelon Center file briefs in cases like this.

key_dates.Court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Texas
key_dates.Outcome timing: Likely 2026-2027 trial-court disposition; appeals to Fifth Circuit likely; possible SCOTUS review
key_dates.Status: Pending; preliminary injunction motions and stays have been litigated

legal_theory: APA + constitutional challenge to HHS Section 504 + Olmstead integration mandate
short_description: Multi-state AG challenge to HHS Section 504 rule's integration mandate. If states win, community-integration rights could narrow.
short_description_professional: Texas, et al. v. Kennedy (formerly Texas v. Becerra), pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas. Multi-state APA + constitutional challenge to HHS's 2024 Section 504 final rule (89 Fed. Reg. 40066, May 9, 2024), including the rule's codification of Olmstead community-integration obligations. If state plaintiffs prevail, federal community-integration protections under § 504 could narrow. Tracking-only listing.
short_description_simple: A group of state attorneys general are suing the federal government. They want to weaken the federal rule that protects the right of people with disabilities to live in their communities (not be forced into nursing homes). If they win, the community-integration protection could get weaker. This is something to watch.

case_name: United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc. (2010 Consent Decree)
court: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia
eligibility:
The consent decree protects guests with disabilities at all Hilton-brand properties nationwide who experience accessible-room or reservation-system barriers.

You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability and require an accessible hotel room.
- You stayed at, tried to book, or had a confirmed reservation at any Hilton-brand property (Hampton, DoubleTree, Homewood, Embassy, Hilton Garden Inn, Hilton, Conrad, Waldorf-Astoria, etc.).
- The reservation system didn't describe accessibility features, didn't let you reserve a specific accessible room, gave away your accessible room to someone else, or otherwise treated accessibility differently than non-accessible reservations.

You don't need to have been part of the original DOJ enforcement to report current violations.
eligibility_professional:
Guests with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who encounter reservation-system or accessible-room access barriers at Hilton-brand properties retain ongoing rights under the consent decree. Coverage extends to all Hilton-owned, -operated, and -franchised brands and properties subject to Hilton's control.

Independent contractor franchisees may have limited liability exposure, but Hilton's reservation-system obligations apply system-wide regardless of property ownership.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at or tried to book any Hilton brand (Hampton, DoubleTree, Embassy, Hilton Garden Inn, etc.).
- The hotel did not let you book a specific accessible room, did not say what makes the room accessible, or gave away your accessible room.

You can report this even though the deal is from 2010 — it is still in effect.
full_description:
This is a federal consent decree between the U.S. Department of Justice and Hilton Worldwide, entered in 2010 in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia (1:10-cv-01902).

The DOJ sued Hilton because guests with mobility disabilities couldn't reserve accessible rooms in the same way other guests could. Many Hilton hotels and brands didn't have proper accessible rooms; when accessible rooms existed, the online and phone reservation systems often couldn't guarantee a specific accessible room, didn't describe what made a room accessible, or offered fewer choices than were available to non-disabled guests.

The consent decree requires Hilton and all of its brands (Hampton Inn, DoubleTree, Homewood Suites, Embassy Suites, Hilton Garden Inn, etc.) to build accessible rooms to the ADA Standards; ensure the reservation system describes accessibility features of each accessible room; allow guests to reserve a SPECIFIC accessible room; hold accessible rooms for guests who request them; and provide accessibility info as detailed as info available about non-accessible rooms.

The consent decree is in long-term compliance phase. This is the consent decree referenced by Niles v. Hilton — Niles is a current case alleging continued bed-height/wheelchair-transfer issues that the 2010 decree didn't directly address.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc., 1:10-cv-01902 (D.D.C., consent decree entered Nov. 9, 2010), is a settled enforcement action under Title III of the ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., addressing Hilton's accessible-room design and reservation-system practices across all Hilton-brand properties (Hampton, DoubleTree, Homewood, Embassy, Garden Inn, Hilton, Conrad, Waldorf-Astoria, etc.).

The consent decree binds Hilton to: (i) construct accessible rooms to ADA Standards; (ii) describe accessibility features of accessible rooms in reservation systems; (iii) permit guests to reserve specific accessible rooms with the same specificity as non-accessible rooms; (iv) hold accessible rooms for guests requesting them; (v) ensure reservation parity in detail and specificity; (vi) train reservation staff; (vii) submit to ongoing monitoring and reporting.

The decree has informed subsequent DOJ regulatory action under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e) (reservation systems requirements) and serves as the template for subsequent hospitality-industry enforcement (Marriott 2024 settlement, etc.).

Compliance phase ongoing. Reports of non-compliance — inaccessible-to-book accessible rooms, missing accessibility descriptions, refusals to honor specific-room reservations — may be made to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), via private Title III actions, or state civil-rights agencies.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Hilton from 2010. It is still in effect.

The DOJ sued Hilton because people with disabilities could not book accessible rooms the same way other people booked regular rooms. Some Hiltons did not have accessible rooms. Some did, but the website would not say which rooms were accessible, or would not let you reserve a specific accessible room.

Hilton agreed to:
- Build accessible rooms.
- Put information about accessible rooms on their website.
- Let you reserve a specific accessible room (not just "an accessible room").
- Hold the room for you so they don't give it to someone else.
- Make accessible-room info as detailed as regular-room info.

This applies to all Hilton brands — Hampton Inn, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hilton Garden Inn, and many others.

If you have trouble booking an accessible room at any Hilton, you can report it. The U.S. Department of Justice will take complaints.

key_dates.Related case: Niles v. Hilton (2026, ongoing — bed-height issues not addressed in 2010 decree)
key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing

short_description: 2010 DOJ consent decree requires Hilton to make rooms accessible and reservation systems compatible. Compliance phase — report current violations that should be covered.
short_description_professional: 2010 DOJ consent decree (1:10-cv-01902, D.D.C.) binding Hilton Worldwide and Hilton-brand properties to accessible-room design and reservation-system parity under ADA Title III. Compliance phase; ongoing enforcement available via DOJ administrative complaint and direct private actions.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Hilton from 2010. Hilton has to make their rooms accessible and make sure people can reserve those rooms online or by phone — just like any other room. If you're having trouble with this at any Hilton, you can report it.

case_name: United States v. Marriott International, Inc. (2024 Settlement)
court: U.S. Department of Justice administrative settlement
eligibility:
The settlement protects guests with disabilities at all Marriott-brand properties nationwide who experience accessible-room or reservation-system barriers.

You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at, tried to book, or had a confirmed reservation at any Marriott-brand property.
- The reservation system didn't let you reserve a specific accessible room, didn't describe accessibility features, gave your room away, or treated accessibility differently than non-accessible reservations.

The settlement is brand-system-wide. It applies whether you booked through Marriott.com, the Bonvoy app, a phone agent, a travel agency, or a third-party site like Expedia or Booking.com.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 encountering reservation-system or accessible-room access barriers at Marriott-portfolio properties retain ongoing rights under the settlement. Coverage applies system-wide regardless of property-level ownership (Marriott corporate-owned, franchise, or third-party operator) where Marriott reservation infrastructure is involved.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at or tried to book any Marriott-brand hotel.
- The hotel did not let you book a specific accessible room, did not say what makes the room accessible, or gave away your accessible room.

Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, Fairfield Inn, SpringHill Suites, Aloft, Element, Renaissance — these are all Marriott brands and all covered.
full_description:
This is a 2024 DOJ settlement with Marriott International. Marriott runs many hotel brands — Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, W, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, SpringHill Suites, Fairfield Inn, AC Hotels, Aloft, Element, Renaissance, Le Méridien, St. Regis, and many more.

DOJ found that Marriott's reservation systems didn't comply with the ADA Standards for accessible-room reservations. Guests with disabilities couldn't reserve specific accessible rooms in the same way other guests could; descriptions of accessibility features were missing or inadequate; specific accessible rooms weren't being held for the guests who requested them; and consistency across websites, mobile apps, and reservation phone lines was inconsistent.

The settlement requires Marriott to allow guests to reserve a specific accessible room with the same specificity available to non-disabled guests; describe each accessible room's features (roll-in shower vs. bathtub with grab bars, communication features for deaf guests, etc.); hold a reserved accessible room for the guest who requested it; train reservation staff; implement parity across all reservation channels (website, app, phone, agency, third-party platforms); and submit to DOJ monitoring.

The settlement covers ALL Marriott brand properties.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Marriott International, Inc. (DOJ administrative settlement, 2024), is a settled enforcement action under Title III ADA addressing reservation-system practices across Marriott's portfolio of brands. The settlement parallels the 2010 Hilton consent decree template and implements ADA Title III obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e).

Binding obligations include: reservation specificity for accessible rooms; comprehensive accessibility-feature descriptions; specific-room holds; reservation channel parity (website, mobile app, phone, GDS, third-party booking platforms); staff training; ongoing DOJ monitoring and reporting.

The settlement covers Marriott's full brand portfolio (Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, W, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, SpringHill Suites, Fairfield Inn, Aloft, Element, Renaissance, Le Méridien, St. Regis, Autograph, Tribute Portfolio, Design Hotels, Edition, Bonvoy properties, etc.).

Compliance phase ongoing. Reports of non-compliance — including failure to honor specific-room reservations, inadequate accessibility descriptions, inter-channel inconsistency, or denial of reservation parity — may be made to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), via private Title III actions, or state civil-rights agencies.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Marriott from 2024.

Marriott runs many hotel brands — Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, Fairfield Inn, Aloft, Element, and more.

DOJ said Marriott was not letting people with disabilities book accessible rooms the same way other people book rooms. Marriott agreed to fix this.

Now Marriott has to:
- Let you reserve a specific accessible room (not just "an accessible room").
- Tell you on the website what makes the room accessible (roll-in shower, grab bars, hearing-impaired features, etc.).
- Hold the room you reserved for you.
- Train staff on accessibility.
- Make the website, mobile app, and phone line all say the same thing.

If you have trouble booking an accessible room at any Marriott brand, you can report it to DOJ at ADA.gov.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing

short_description: 2024 DOJ settlement requires Marriott's accessible-room reservation system to permit equal reservation. Report current violations.
short_description_professional: 2024 DOJ settlement agreement binding Marriott International and its brand portfolio to accessible-room reservation-system reforms under ADA Title III. Compliance phase; ongoing enforcement via DOJ administrative complaint.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Marriott from 2024. Marriott has to fix its accessible-room booking system at all its hotels. If you can't book an accessible room at any Marriott brand, you can report it.

case_name: K-12 school district digital inaccessibility
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You're a parent or guardian of a K-12 student in any US public school district or charter school, OR you're an adult student in a public K-12 setting, OR you're a parent/guardian with a disability whose child's school system isn't accessible to YOU.
- The student (or parent) has a disability — any kind.
- A digital tool used by the school district or required by the curriculum has accessibility barriers.

Coverage extends to public school districts and charter schools; for private schools, Section 504 applies if they receive federal funding and Title III applies if they're a place of public accommodation.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to: K-12 students with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102, 29 U.S.C. § 705(9)(B), or 20 U.S.C. § 1401(3); parents/guardians with qualifying disabilities encountering inaccessible district-facing systems; and prospective students (e.g., enrollment-system accessibility). Title II applies to public schools and charter schools as state actors; Section 504 applies to all federally funded institutions; IDEA's overlay applies to students with IEPs and 504 plans.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- Your child is a student in a US public school (or charter school).
- OR you're a parent with a disability.
- A school computer tool, website, or app isn't accessible.
full_description:
Public school districts and charter schools are state actors under ADA Title II and recipients of federal funding under Section 504 — both of which require digital accessibility. The DOJ April 2024 Title II final rule explicitly applies to school districts with WCAG 2.1 AA conformance deadlines (April 24, 2026 for districts serving 50,000+ residents).

Common K-12 digital accessibility violations: district websites with inaccessible enrollment, lunch payments, or forms; parent/guardian portals (PowerSchool, Skyward, Infinite Campus) that don't work with screen readers; learning platforms (Google Classroom, Schoology, Seesaw, Clever) with failures; homework systems (IXL, Khan Academy, ALEKS) that don't work with assistive technology; video lessons without captions; scanned-image PDF assignments; online testing without accommodation supports; and communication apps (ClassDojo, Remind, Bloomz) inaccessible to deaf or low-vision parents.

The legal framework: ADA Title II + Section 504 govern accessibility for students AND parents/guardians; IDEA creates additional obligations for students with IEPs and 504 plans, including accessible instructional materials; AIM/AEM standards apply for students with print disabilities.

Where to report: the district's 504 coordinator; the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights (the primary K-12 enforcer) at ed.gov/ocr; DOJ Civil Rights Division; the State Department of Education; and State Protection & Advocacy.
full_description_professional:
K-12 digital accessibility is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35); Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794, 34 C.F.R. Part 104); IDEA (20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq., 34 C.F.R. Part 300); and the Accessible Educational Materials provisions under IDEA. The DOJ April 2024 Title II final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) explicitly extends WCAG 2.1 AA compliance obligations to public school districts with deadlines of April 24, 2026 (large entities) and April 26, 2027 (smaller entities).

Substantive obligations span: (i) general public-facing accessibility for parents/community; (ii) student-facing instructional content accessibility, with augmented obligations under IDEA's FAPE (Free Appropriate Public Education) standard and Section 504's "equal opportunity" standard; (iii) AIM/AEM provision for students with print disabilities under IDEA's NIMAS framework; (iv) communication-access for deaf parents and students; (v) accommodation-supported online testing.

Enforcement avenues: institutional § 504 coordinator complaint (creates notice, exhausts administrative remedies); ED OCR (primary K-12 enforcement; ed.gov/ocr); DOJ Civil Rights Division (28 C.F.R. § 35.170); state DOE compliance offices; state P&A entities; private § 504 actions and IDEA Due Process Hearings for individual relief.
full_description_simple:
Schools and school districts have to make their websites and computer tools accessible to kids with disabilities AND parents with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The school website parent portal doesn't work with your screen reader.
- Your child's learning platform (Google Classroom, Schoology, etc.) won't work with their assistive technology.
- Homework apps (IXL, Khan Academy, etc.) aren't accessible.
- Videos in class have no captions for a deaf student.
- The PDF homework is a picture you can't read.
- Online testing for state tests doesn't accommodate disabilities.
- Class apps (ClassDojo, Remind) don't work for blind parents.

April 2026 deadline: large school districts now have to comply with accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1 AA). Smaller districts have until April 2027.

Where to report:
- The school district's 504 coordinator or special education office FIRST. This creates a record.
- U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights at ed.gov/ocr — this is the main place for school complaints.
- Your state Department of Education.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office.
short_description: School district websites, student portals, learning platforms, or homework systems that aren't accessible.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II + Section 504 + IDEA framework for K-12 digital accessibility. DOJ's April 2024 Title II final rule (WCAG 2.1 AA, April 2026 deadline for large entities) explicitly covers public school districts. Common violations: inaccessible district websites, student/parent portals, learning platforms (Google Classroom, Schoology, Seesaw, etc.), homework systems, and online testing.
short_description_simple: Your child's school district website, parent portal, learning platform, or homework system doesn't work for kids (or parents) with disabilities. School districts are required by federal law to make these accessible. April 2026 DOJ deadline applies to large districts.

case_name: Restaurant and business service animal denials
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You use a trained service animal (a dog trained to perform work or tasks because of your disability).
- A restaurant, store, hotel, hospital, mall, theater, gym, taxi, or other public-accommodation business refused you entry, kicked you out, demanded documentation, required a vest, charged you extra, or otherwise treated you differently because of your service animal.

NOT eligible under this framework: emotional support animals (covered under FHA for housing, not ADA for public accommodations); pets and untrained companion animals. For Uber/Lyft specifically, see the rideshare listing.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to handlers of dogs (and miniature horses under § 36.302(c)(9)) trained to perform work or tasks for persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102. Coverage does NOT extend to emotional support animals (covered under FHA and ACAA, not ADA Title III) or untrained companion animals.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- Your dog is a trained service animal (not an emotional support animal — those have different rules).
- A restaurant, store, hotel, or other business wouldn't let you in (or kicked you out) because of your dog.
full_description:
Service-animal access is one of the most clearly established areas of ADA law. Under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c), public accommodations (restaurants, stores, hotels, hospitals, malls, theaters, gyms, taxis, and many others) must permit service animals to accompany their handlers in all areas open to the general public.

The law is clear: staff may ask only TWO questions — (1) Is the dog a service animal required because of a disability? (2) What work or task has it been trained to perform? Staff may NOT ask for documentation, a vest, an ID, or a demonstration. Allergies and fear of dogs are NOT valid reasons to deny access. "No pets" policies do not apply to service animals. Cleaning fees specifically for service animals are illegal. Service animals must be permitted in food preparation areas.

NOT covered: emotional support animals (covered under FHA for housing, not ADA for public accommodations); pets and untrained companion animals; animals other than dogs (miniature horses have limited additional coverage).

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; the business's corporate accessibility office; the state attorney general; for California, CRD for Unruh Act statutory damages ($4,000 per offense); for NYC, NYCCHR; and disability-rights organizations (NFB, NAGDU).
full_description_professional:
Service-animal access is governed by ADA Title III, 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c), and parallel Title II provisions. The framework establishes a near-absolute right of access for service-animal handlers to all areas open to the public at Title III public accommodations, subject only to legitimate safety concerns articulated under § 36.208 (safety threats based on individualized assessment) and § 36.302(c)(2) (housebreaking/under-control requirements).

Permissible inquiries are strictly limited to 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c)(6): (1) whether the dog is required because of a disability; (2) what work or task the dog has been trained to perform. Documentation, identification, and task-demonstration requirements are impermissible. Allergies, fear, religious objection, and "no pets" policies are not cognizable defenses. Surcharges or cleaning fees specifically targeting service animals are barred by § 36.301(c).

Service animals as defined under 28 C.F.R. § 36.104 are limited to dogs (with limited additional miniature-horse coverage under § 36.302(c)(9)) individually trained to perform work or tasks for a person with a disability. Emotional support animals are not service animals under Title III but may be covered under Fair Housing Act and Air Carrier Access Act regimes.

State law parallels: California Unruh Civil Rights Act ($4,000 statutory damages per offense); NYCHRL; Massachusetts PAA; and others provide stronger remedies than Title III's injunctive-only relief. The litigation pattern is well-established with thousands of resolved cases.

Reporting: DOJ § 36.502 administrative complaint; state AG and state civil-rights agency for statutory-damages states; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; coordination with NAGDU, NFB, Guide Dogs for the Blind, and similar handler advocacy organizations.
full_description_simple:
A restaurant, store, hotel, hospital, or other business cannot kick you out because of your service animal. The ADA protects you.

What the law says:
- A service animal can go almost anywhere the public can go.
- Staff can only ask you TWO things:
  - Is the dog a service animal because of a disability?
  - What is the dog trained to do?
- Staff CAN'T ask for paperwork, a vest, a special ID, or to see the dog do its task.
- "No pets allowed" doesn't apply to service animals.
- Allergies are NOT a reason to deny you. The business has to accommodate both you and the person with allergies.
- They CAN'T charge you extra cleaning fees for the service animal.

NOT covered:
- Emotional support animals (ESAs) are NOT service animals. They have housing protection but not restaurant or store protection.
- Other pets.
- Animals other than dogs (some special rules for small horses but very limited).

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The business's corporate office.
- Your state attorney general.
- In California: file with CRD — you can get $4,000 per incident.
- In NYC: file with NYC Commission on Human Rights.
- For Uber and Lyft, see the rideshare listing.

key_dates.Status: Well-established law; ongoing individual enforcement

short_description: A restaurant, store, or rideshare refused to serve you with your service animal. No current class action — most cases are individual. Tell us what happened.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III service-animal access. 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c) prohibits public accommodations from refusing service to handlers with service animals. Permissible inquiries are limited to two questions (whether the dog is required because of a disability; what work or task it's trained to perform). Allergies, fear, and "no pets" policies are NOT defenses. Statutory damages available in many states via Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA, etc.
short_description_simple: A restaurant, store, hotel, or other business refused to let you in with your service animal — or kicked you out. This is illegal under the ADA. Report it.

case_name: Pharmacy and retail self-service kiosk inaccessibility
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability — blindness, low vision, deafness, hard-of-hearing, motor impairment, cognitive disability, or other.
- You encountered a kiosk you couldn't use because of an accessibility barrier.
- The kiosk was at a place open to the public (pharmacy, restaurant, airport, hospital, government office, retail, etc.).

You don't have to have made a purchase or completed the transaction.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 encountering inaccessible self-service kiosks in any place of public accommodation (Title III) or government-operated facility (Title II). Standing under Title III is established by access denial; completion of the underlying transaction is not required.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability.
- A kiosk somewhere (pharmacy, restaurant, airport, etc.) wouldn't work for you.
full_description:
Self-service kiosks are increasingly common — and increasingly inaccessible to people with disabilities. ADA Title III requires these devices to be usable by all customers, including those who are blind, low-vision, deaf, hard-of-hearing, or have motor impairments.

Common violations: touchscreen-only interfaces with no tactile keypad or audio output; text too small or low contrast; visual-only confirmations; no headphone jack for private audio output; kiosks mounted too high for wheelchair users; and required tasks (payment, signature, PIN entry) that can't be done without disclosing private information to a third party.

Contexts where this matters: pharmacy POS terminals (blind users forced to disclose prescriptions or payment info verbally); restaurant ordering kiosks; airport check-in; hospital and clinic check-in (confidential medical info exposed); government service kiosks; and retail self-checkout.

The legal framework: ADA Title III for public accommodations; Title II for government kiosks (DMV, courts); Section 508 for federal kiosks. DOJ has signaled enforcement intent but has not issued kiosk-specific regulations.

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; the business directly; the state attorney general; and disability-rights legal organizations (DRA, NFB, NDRN, ACB).
full_description_professional:
Self-service kiosk accessibility is governed primarily by ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for public-accommodation operators and Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for government-operated devices. Section 508 (29 U.S.C. § 794d) governs federal-government kiosks specifically.

Substantive requirements derive from Title III's effective-communication mandate (28 C.F.R. § 36.303), policy-and-procedure modification obligation (§ 36.302(a)), and reasonable-accommodation mandate. The 2010 ADA Standards for Accessible Design include some kiosk-specific provisions (Section 707 for ATMs; analogous principles applied to other transaction machines).

Litigation has established the pattern: NFB v. Caesar's Entertainment (gambling-floor kiosks); NFB v. Apple Store (self-checkout); multiple settlements with pharmacy chains (CVS, Walgreens point-of-sale equipment); restaurant chain settlements (Panera Bread kiosk accessibility); airport check-in litigation. DOJ has signaled increased enforcement priority for kiosk accessibility.

Reporting and enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint; state AG consumer-protection/civil-rights enforcement; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; coordination with NFB, ACB, NDRN, DRA for systemic litigation.
full_description_simple:
Self-service kiosks — the screens you use to order food, check in at the airport, pay at the pharmacy, or do other things — often don't work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The pharmacy kiosk needs you to read a touch screen — you can't if you're blind.
- The McDonald's ordering kiosk has tiny text — you can't read it with low vision.
- The airport check-in kiosk needs you to see what's on the screen — there's no audio.
- The kiosk is too high for your wheelchair.
- The kiosk needs you to type in a PIN or password — you can't hide it from people watching.

The law:
- The ADA says public-accommodation kiosks must be usable by people with disabilities.

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Tell the business directly — get the manager's name.
- Your state attorney general.
- Disability rights organizations like the National Federation of the Blind (nfb.org).

key_dates.Notable litigation: NFB v. Caesar's (settled), NFB v. Apple Store (settled), pharmacy POS settlements
key_dates.Status: Active individual + systemic litigation; no comprehensive DOJ kiosk rule yet

short_description: Self-service kiosks at pharmacies, restaurants, airports, or government offices that aren't usable by blind, low-vision, or deaf customers.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III kiosk accessibility. Common targets: pharmacy POS terminals (CVS, Walgreens, etc.), restaurant ordering kiosks (McDonald's, fast-casual), airport check-in kiosks, hospital and clinic check-in, government-service kiosks, retail self-checkout. Litigation is largely individual but pattern is established (e.g., NFB v. Caesar's, settlement re point-of-sale equipment). DOJ has signaled increased enforcement.
short_description_simple: Self-service kiosks — at pharmacies, restaurants, airports, hospitals, government offices, retail stores, and more — often can't be used by blind, low-vision, or deaf customers. This is illegal under the ADA. Report violations.

case_name: State DMV and government online services inaccessibility
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability — vision, hearing, motor, cognitive, or other.
- You tried to use a state or local government website or mobile app.
- The site or app didn't work with your assistive technology OR had barriers (unlabeled buttons, captchas you can't solve, video without captions, etc.).

Coverage: ALL state and local government services — DMV, courts, benefits, taxes, parks/recreation, public health, voter registration, public schools, libraries, transit agencies, and more.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to all persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 encountering Title II covered entity web or mobile content that fails WCAG 2.1 AA conformance or fails to provide effective access through alternative means.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability.
- You couldn't use a state or local government website.

This covers anything run by state or local government: DMV, courts, benefits, schools, libraries, parks, transit, taxes — anything.
full_description:
State and local government websites and online services — DMV, courts, benefits portals (SNAP, Medicaid, unemployment), tax systems, voter registration, and dozens of others — are required by federal law to be accessible.

In April 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice finalized a rule under ADA Title II that explicitly requires state and local governments to make their web content and mobile apps conform to WCAG 2.1 AA. The compliance deadlines are April 24, 2026 for entities serving 50,000 or more, and April 26, 2027 for smaller entities and special-purpose districts.

If you're a person with a disability and you can't use a state or local government website — your screen reader can't read the form, the captcha blocks you, the PDF is image-only, the video has no captions, the buttons aren't labeled, the mobile app crashes with VoiceOver — that's a Title II violation. The largest jurisdictions are already required to comply.

Where to report: DOJ at ADA.gov (free); the state attorney general's civil rights division; the state Protection & Advocacy entity; and for DMVs and courts specifically, the state disability advocacy office.
full_description_professional:
DOJ's final rule at 28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq., effective April 24, 2024 (compliance deadlines April 24, 2026 for entities serving populations of 50,000+ and all states; April 26, 2027 for entities under 50,000 and special purpose districts), explicitly requires state and local government web content and mobile apps to conform to WCAG 2.1 AA.

Substantive requirements: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for all web content under entity control; mobile-application accessibility; remediation of legacy content; auxiliary-aids provision for assistive-technology users; ongoing monitoring and complaint-resolution procedures.

The rule applies to all Title II covered entities (state and local governments and their instrumentalities). Implementation will trigger sustained private litigation and DOJ enforcement; early enforcement priorities include voter-facing services, public benefits portals, and DMV systems.

Reporting and enforcement: DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170; state attorney general civil-rights divisions; state Protection & Advocacy entities; private Title II actions for injunctive relief and fees under 42 U.S.C. § 12205. For voter-facing services, parallel HAVA enforcement is available.
full_description_simple:
Government websites need to work for everyone — including people with disabilities. State DMVs, courts, benefits portals, and other government websites have to be accessible by law.

In 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice made this an official, enforceable rule with deadlines:
- April 2026: All states and any local government serving 50,000+ people must comply.
- April 2027: Smaller cities and counties must comply.

If you couldn't use a government website because you're blind, low vision, deaf, or have a learning or motor disability — that's a violation.

Examples of violations:
- DMV renewal form doesn't work with screen readers.
- Court e-filing system has unlabeled buttons.
- Unemployment portal video has no captions.
- Benefits application is an image PDF you can't read.
- State tax website crashes with VoiceOver.

Report it:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Your state attorney general.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office (Google "[your state] Protection and Advocacy").

key_dates.Status: Active enforcement; April 2026 deadline now in effect for large entities

legal_theory: ADA Title II + DOJ Title II Web Rule (April 2026 compliance deadline for large entities)
short_description: DMV websites, benefits portals, court systems, or state agency online services that block access for screen reader users. April 2026 DOJ deadline now active for the largest jurisdictions.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II web accessibility — DOJ's April 24, 2024 final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) sets enforceable WCAG 2.1 AA compliance deadlines for state and local government digital services. April 24, 2026 deadline applies to entities with populations of 50,000+ (and all states); April 26, 2027 for entities under 50,000.
short_description_simple: The DMV, court system, benefits portal, or state agency website you needed to use doesn't work with your screen reader. DOJ has a NEW rule (April 2026 deadline for large jurisdictions) requiring state and local government websites to be accessible.

case_name: Rideshare wheelchair and service animal denials
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability — specifically service-animal use, wheelchair/mobility-device use, or other.
- You used (or tried to use) Uber, Lyft, or another rideshare service.
- A driver refused you, cancelled after seeing your service animal or mobility device, was unable to accommodate, charged you improperly, or otherwise treated you differently.

For Uber service-animal refusals specifically, the NFB-CA v. Uber settlement is in effect and your report directly enforces it.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 using or attempting to use any rideshare/TNC platform subject to Title III. For Uber service-animal refusals specifically, the NFB-CA v. Uber settlement (3:14-cv-04086) provides additional enforcement mechanisms. State-law parallels (Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA) provide statutory damages.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability (service animal, wheelchair, scooter, walker, blindness, deafness, etc.).
- You used or tried to use Uber, Lyft, or similar.
- The driver refused you, charged you extra, or couldn't serve you.
full_description:
Uber, Lyft, and other rideshare services are bound by the ADA's public-accommodation requirements. Multiple ongoing matters address common patterns.

Service animal refusals: a driver refused you, drove away on seeing your service animal, or asked you to leave your dog behind. This is what NFB-CA v. Uber settled in 2016 — the settlement makes Uber permanently remove drivers who knowingly refuse and requires Uber to track all such complaints. Lyft is in separate ongoing litigation.

Wheelchair-accessible vehicle (WAV) access: Uber and Lyft are required to provide wheelchair-accessible service where they operate. In many cities WAV service is significantly slower, more expensive, or unavailable — a "service desert" that doesn't exist for non-disabled users. WAV waits of 30+ minutes (vs. 3-5 for standard rides) are common, and multiple lawsuits are pending.

Improper cleaning fees: fees charged specifically for service animals are illegal, as are fees for "mess" attributed to a service animal that didn't make one.

Where to report: for Uber, in-app reporting (required by settlement), the NFB testing program, plus DOJ; for Lyft, in-app reporting plus DOJ; DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; the state attorney general (especially CA, NY, MA); and Disability Rights Advocates at dralegal.org.
full_description_professional:
Rideshare accessibility is governed by ADA Title III for transportation services with DOT-regulation overlay where applicable (49 C.F.R. Part 37 for transportation-network-company analogs may apply in specific contexts; see also state PUC/TNC regulatory frameworks).

Active litigation and settlements:
- NFB-CA v. Uber, 3:14-cv-04086 (N.D. Cal., 2016 settlement; see related listing) established nationwide service-animal enforcement obligations: permanent platform removal for knowing refusals, complaint-tracking, NFB testing program, retired-judge monitor. Court retains jurisdiction.
- Ongoing Lyft service-animal litigation in parallel.
- WAV access: multiple suits against Uber and Lyft in major metros alleging unequal service availability for wheelchair users — Equal Rights Center actions, Crawford v. Uber-style claims, etc. Resolution patterns include WAV-supply requirements and waiting-time parity.
- Improper-fee litigation: cleaning fees specifically charged for service-animal accommodations are barred by 28 C.F.R. § 36.301(c).

Substantive obligations: equal service access under Title III; reasonable modifications under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(a); effective communication for deaf riders; service-animal access per § 36.302(c) (driver allergies, fear, religious objection NOT defenses); prohibition on disability surcharges per § 36.301(c).

State-law overlays: California Unruh Act ($4,000 statutory damages per offense, particularly leveraged in this area); NYCHRL; Massachusetts PAA; state TNC regulations may impose additional accessibility obligations.

Enforcement: in-app reporting (NFB-CA v. Uber settlement requires this); NFB testing program (specifically Uber-focused); DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint; state AG civil-rights divisions; private Title III + state-law counsel for monetary-damages claims; DRA for systemic litigation.
full_description_simple:
Uber and Lyft have to give you rides if you have a service animal or use a wheelchair. They can't charge you extra.

Common problems:
- A driver sees your service animal and drives away.
- A driver says "I'm allergic" and refuses you.
- You can't get a wheelchair-accessible vehicle (WAV), or you wait 30+ minutes for one when other people wait 3 minutes.
- You get charged a cleaning fee because of your service dog (this is illegal).
- A driver asks for proof of your disability (also illegal).

There are active lawsuits about this:
- Uber had a big case (NFB-CA v. Uber) that settled in 2016. Uber drivers who knowingly refuse get banned from the platform. (See the related listing.)
- Lyft is currently being sued for similar things.
- Both companies are being sued over wheelchair-accessible vehicle service.

Where to report:
- Report through the Uber or Lyft app first (creates a record).
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- NFB testing program (for Uber service-animal cases especially).
- Your state attorney general.
- Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org.
- If you're in California, NY, or MA, also file with your state civil rights agency.

key_dates.Status: Active multi-track enforcement; particularly strong leverage for Uber service-animal claims via NFB settlement

legal_theory: ADA Title III + state civil rights laws
short_description: Uber or Lyft drivers refused your service animal, failed to provide a wheelchair-accessible vehicle, or charged improper fees. Multiple active cases — your story helps build the pattern.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III rideshare access. Service-animal refusal: NFB-CA v. Uber settled with binding nationwide policy (see related listing); ongoing Lyft litigation. Wheelchair-accessible vehicle (WAV) access: pending litigation against both Uber and Lyft over service deserts. Improper cleaning fees: pattern of complaints. State-law parallels (Unruh, NYCHRL) provide statutory damages.
short_description_simple: An Uber or Lyft driver refused you because of your service animal, couldn't provide a wheelchair-accessible vehicle, OR charged you improper cleaning fees. Multiple active lawsuits already exist — your story helps. Report it.

case_name: Polling place and ballot inaccessibility (general)
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You're a registered voter (or eligible-but-not-registered voter) in any US state.
- You have a disability — mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, or other.
- You encountered a voting-access barrier: polling place wasn't accessible, voting equipment didn't work, curbside refused, mail ballot inaccessible, etc.

Coverage extends to general, primary, special, municipal, and tribal elections, and voter registration access.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 in any US jurisdiction encountering Title II/HAVA-implicating voting access barriers. Voter-registration access (NVRA framework, 52 U.S.C. § 20503) is also covered.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You vote (or want to vote) anywhere in the US.
- You have a disability.
- Something at the polling place or with your ballot didn't work because of your disability.
full_description:
Voting access for people with disabilities is one of the most active areas of federal civil-rights enforcement. Federal law requires physical access to polling places (ADA Title II + ADA Standards); at least one accessible voting machine per polling place (HAVA); curbside voting for voters who can't safely enter; accessible mail-in or absentee ballots where mail voting is offered; and poll worker training on disability access.

Despite these requirements, barriers remain widespread: polling places at inaccessible churches, schools, or community centers; accessible voting machines that aren't deployed, set up, or functional; poll workers unfamiliar with the equipment; curbside voting refused or unstaffed; paper-only mail ballots requiring sighted assistance for blind voters; and audio-ballot equipment that's broken or set up in conspicuous locations destroying ballot privacy.

For specific jurisdictions with active matters, see Bryant v. Harris County (TX), DOJ v. LA County, and the DOJ investigation of Alaska Division of Elections. For voting problems in OTHER jurisdictions, this listing covers your reporting options.

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section at ADA.gov or voting.section@usdoj.gov; your state Secretary of State; your state Protection & Advocacy; your state attorney general; and the Election Protection coalition (866-OUR-VOTE).
full_description_professional:
Voter access for persons with disabilities is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35, including § 35.151 ADA Standards for polling places); the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081 (accessible voting equipment requirements; one accessible voting system per polling place); Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794) for federally funded electoral activity; the Voting Rights Act, 52 U.S.C. §§ 10301 et seq. (general disability-related provisions); and the National Voter Registration Act (motor-voter accessibility).

Substantive obligations span:
- Polling-place physical access under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and the 2010 ADA Standards.
- HAVA-mandated accessible voting equipment deployment (52 U.S.C. § 21081), with one accessible system per polling place.
- Effective communication for deaf voters at polling places (28 C.F.R. § 35.160).
- Mail-ballot accessibility under Title II's equal-access standard (the Bryant v. Harris County theory).
- Curbside voting under state-law mandates and Title II program-access.

For specific active jurisdiction matters: Bryant v. Harris County (S.D. Tex.) — mail ballot RAVBM; DOJ v. LA County (settled August 2024) — vote-center accessibility; DOJ investigation of Alaska Division of Elections (ongoing). This listing covers other jurisdictions.

Enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (highly active enforcement area); state Secretary of State (HAVA administration); state P&A (election-protection capacity); Election Protection coalition (1-866-OUR-VOTE, real-time election-day support); private Title II actions; § 1983 actions for individual constitutional voting-rights violations.
full_description_simple:
Voting needs to be accessible to people with disabilities. Federal law has very strong protections.

Federal law says:
- Polling places must be wheelchair accessible.
- Every polling place must have at least one accessible voting machine.
- Curbside voting must be offered if you can't go inside.
- If your state lets people vote by mail, those ballots must be accessible to print-disabled voters too.

But problems are still common:
- The polling place is up steps with no ramp.
- The accessible voting machine isn't plugged in, or no one knows how to set it up.
- Curbside voting isn't offered or staff doesn't come outside.
- Paper mail ballots can't be read by blind voters with screen readers.

If your specific state/county has an active case, check our case list (Bryant v. Harris County for Texas, DOJ v. LA County for LA, the Alaska investigation).

If your problem is somewhere else:
- File a complaint with DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Call DOJ Voting Section: voting.section@usdoj.gov.
- Tell your state Secretary of State.
- Tell Election Protection: 866-OUR-VOTE (866-687-8683).
- Tell your state Protection & Advocacy office.

key_dates.Status: Active continuous enforcement

short_description: Inaccessible polling place, broken accessible voting machine, no curbside option, or inaccessible vote-by-mail. For cases not already covered by Bryant v. Harris County or DOJ v. LA County.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II + HAVA + Section 504 framework for polling-place and ballot accessibility. Common violations: physical access barriers, accessible-voting-machine deployment/operability failures, curbside-voting refusals, inaccessible mail-in ballots for print-disabled voters. For jurisdiction-specific active cases see Bryant v. Harris County (TX mail-ballot RAVBM) and DOJ v. LA County (vote-center accessibility); this listing covers other jurisdictions.
short_description_simple: Your polling place isn't accessible, the voting machine doesn't work, you weren't offered curbside voting, OR you couldn't mark a mail-in ballot independently. This is illegal under multiple federal laws. Report it.

case_name: Mobile app and banking app screen reader incompatibility
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a vision disability (blindness or low vision) — or other disability — that requires assistive technology to use mobile apps.
- You tried to use a mobile app for a financial service, retail, food, ride, or other consumer-facing service.
- The app didn't work with your assistive technology (VoiceOver, TalkBack, screen magnifier, switch control, voice control, etc.).

You don't have to have completed a transaction. If you're in New York, California, Massachusetts, or another state with strong parallel laws, your remedies may be significantly stronger including monetary damages.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 using mobile applications for services covered by Title III's public-accommodation framework. Standing is established by access denial; transaction completion is not required. State-law parallel coverage (NY, CA, MA, etc.) may provide statutory damages unavailable under Title III alone.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You use a screen reader (or other assistive tech) on your phone.
- You tried to use an app — bank, store, food, rideshare, etc.
- The app didn't work for you.

If you live in California, New York, or Massachusetts, the law there is even stronger and you might get money damages.
full_description:
Mobile apps — especially banking apps, retail apps, food delivery, rideshare, and government-service apps — frequently don't work with iPhone's VoiceOver or Android's TalkBack. This makes them unusable for blind and low-vision people.

This is an emerging area of disability-rights law. Courts have generally held that mobile apps are covered by ADA Title III when there's a "nexus" between the app and a physical place of public accommodation. Coverage of pure-online services is more contested, but the trend is toward broader coverage.

Common violations: unlabeled buttons (VoiceOver says "Button" instead of "Transfer Money"); inaccessible drag-and-drop; inaccessible CAPTCHAs; image-only content with no alt text; custom controls that don't expose state; mandatory tutorials that block screen-reader users; and authentication systems that don't work with assistive technology.

The legal framework: ADA Title III (public-accommodation coverage); Section 504 (if federally funded); and state laws (New York GBL § 349, California Unruh Act, Massachusetts Public Accommodation Act) that provide stronger remedies including monetary damages. DOJ has signaled enforcement intent. Notable litigation: NFB v. PNC Bank (pending), NFB v. Bank of America (settled), and numerous retail/food-app cases.

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; the company's accessibility office; the state attorney general; the state civil-rights agency (NY, CA, MA); NFB and ACB; and private counsel.
full_description_professional:
Mobile-application accessibility is an emerging area of ADA Title III litigation, with Title III coverage of mobile apps generally established through the "nexus theory" linking digital products to underlying physical public accommodations (Robles v. Domino's, 913 F.3d 898 (9th Cir. 2019); cert. denied, 140 S. Ct. 122 (2019)). The 11th Circuit's Gil v. Winn-Dixie precedent went the other direction, creating circuit split, though many post-Robles decisions have followed Robles's reasoning.

Substantive WCAG 2.1/2.2 success criteria implicate: 1.1.1 (alt text), 1.3.1 (info and relationships), 1.4.3 (contrast), 2.1.1 (keyboard accessibility, including for switch users), 2.4.7 (focus visibility), 4.1.2 (ARIA name/role/value), and mobile-specific criteria around touch targets (2.5.5) and motion-activated alternatives (2.5.4).

Concurrent state-law frameworks provide stronger remedies: New York General Business Law § 349 (deceptive-practices framework, statutory damages); California Unruh Civil Rights Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§ 51 & 52 ($4,000 per offense statutory damages); Massachusetts Public Accommodation Act; New York City Human Rights Law (NYCHRL § 8-107).

Notable litigation: NFB v. PNC Bank (ongoing); NFB v. Bank of America (settled); financial-services and retail-app dockets are extensive.

Enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division (Title III, 28 C.F.R. § 36.502); state AG civil-rights divisions; state P&A; private Title III counsel for nexus-theory cases; state-law parallel actions for statutory damages where available; coordination with NFB, ACB, NDRN, DRA for systemic pattern documentation.
full_description_simple:
Apps on your phone — like banking apps, store apps, food delivery apps — often don't work for blind people.

The screen reader on your phone (VoiceOver on iPhone, TalkBack on Android) reads what's on the screen out loud. But when an app isn't designed right, the screen reader says "Button" instead of telling you what the button does. Or the app shows your bank balance as a picture instead of text. Or the buttons need you to drag instead of tap.

This area of law is still being figured out by courts. But many cases have already been settled or are in progress:
- Bank of America settled.
- PNC Bank case is pending.
- Many retail and food delivery apps have been sued.

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The company's customer service or accessibility office.
- Your state attorney general.
- The National Federation of the Blind (nfb.org).
- The American Council of the Blind (acb.org).

key_dates.Status: Emerging litigation area; multiple pending cases; circuit split on nexus requirement

legal_theory: ADA Title III (nexus theory) + state law
short_description: Banking apps, retail apps, or service apps unusable with VoiceOver or TalkBack. Emerging area of litigation.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III mobile-app accessibility under the "nexus" theory connecting digital products to physical place of public accommodation. Active litigation: NFB v. PNC Bank, NFB v. Bank of America (settled), and dozens of pending cases against banks, retailers, food service, and other consumer-facing app operators. State law (NY GBL § 349, CA Unruh) provides additional remedies.
short_description_simple: Your banking app, retail app, or service app doesn't work with VoiceOver (iPhone) or TalkBack (Android). This is an emerging area of disability rights law — multiple lawsuits are pending. Report the company.

case_name: Higher education online learning inaccessibility
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You're a student (current, prospective, or recent) at a college, university, or graduate program in the US.
- You have a disability — vision, hearing, motor, cognitive, learning, mental health, chronic illness, or other.
- You encountered a digital barrier to your education: course materials, LMS, video lectures, testing, online communication, lab simulations, etc.

You don't need to be a registered disability services student. The institutional obligation exists independent of registration.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to current, prospective, and recent students with qualifying disabilities at any US institution of higher education subject to Title II, Title III, or § 504 jurisdiction. Disability-services-office registration is not a prerequisite for external complaint filing or for class membership in private litigation, though registration may be relevant to individualized accommodation-history claims.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You're a student at a US college or university.
- You have any kind of disability.
- A digital tool at your school didn't work for you.

You don't need to be officially registered with disability services to file a complaint.
full_description:
College and graduate-school online learning has barriers for students with disabilities everywhere. Federal law requires accessibility, and DOJ has been increasingly active in enforcement.

Common violations: learning management systems (Canvas, Blackboard, Moodle, D2L) with unlabeled buttons, broken keyboard navigation, or screen-reader failures; course videos without captions or transcripts; scanned-image-only PDFs; online proctoring software that doesn't work for blind or motor-impaired students or that flags disability-related behavior as "cheating"; testing platforms that don't accommodate extended time; STEM-specific failures (math as images, inaccessible lab simulations); and inaccessible communication channels.

The legal framework: public colleges fall under Title II + Section 504; private colleges under Title III + Section 504 (almost all receive federal funding via student aid). DOJ's 2023 Dear Colleague Letter addressed higher-ed digital accessibility, and the 2024 Title II web rule applies to public institutions with WCAG 2.1 AA deadlines.

Where to report: your school's disability services office; DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; HHS Office for Civil Rights; the U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights; State Protection & Advocacy; and private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN) for systemic issues.
full_description_professional:
Higher-education digital accessibility is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for public institutions; Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for private institutions of public accommodation; Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794 and 34 C.F.R. Part 104) for all federally funded institutions; Section 1557 of the ACA (42 U.S.C. § 18116) for healthcare-program intersection.

The DOJ 2023 Dear Colleague Letter on Digital Accessibility in Higher Education provides interpretive guidance. The April 2024 DOJ Title II final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) imposes specific WCAG 2.1 AA compliance deadlines on public institutions: April 24, 2026 for large entities (50,000+ population served, and all state institutions); April 26, 2027 for smaller entities.

Common substantive issues litigated: LMS accessibility (NFB v. UC Berkeley, NFB v. MIT, et seq.); auto-captioning quality and live captioning provision; image-based PDFs and inaccessible STEM content; proctoring-software discrimination (post-2020 surge of complaints); testing-time-accommodation enforcement.

Enforcement avenues: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov, 28 C.F.R. § 35.170); HHS OCR (§ 504 enforcement); Department of Education OCR (education-specific § 504 + Title II enforcement, ed.gov/ocr); state Protection & Advocacy entities; private rights of action for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees under 42 U.S.C. § 12205 and 29 U.S.C. § 794a(b).
full_description_simple:
Colleges and universities use computer tools to teach. Some of these tools don't work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The Canvas or Blackboard site doesn't work with your screen reader.
- The professor's videos have no captions.
- The course PDFs are pictures of text — your screen reader can't read them.
- The online test software won't let you use your assistive technology, or marks you as a cheater because of your disability.
- Math equations are images.
- Zoom classes don't have captions.

The law:
- Public colleges (state schools): must comply with the ADA AND Section 504.
- Private colleges: also must comply with the ADA and Section 504 (because almost all take federal money).

Where to report:
- Your school's disability services office — file an internal complaint first. It creates a record.
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights — they handle education-specific complaints.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights (also covers federally funded schools).
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office.

key_dates.Status: Active enforcement; deadlines now in effect for large public institutions

legal_theory: ADA Title II/III + Section 504 + DOJ Dear Colleague Letter (2023)
short_description: Inaccessible LMS, course materials, videos without captions, or online testing platforms at colleges and universities.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II (public colleges) / Title III (private colleges) + Section 504 governance of higher-education digital accessibility. DOJ's 2023 Dear Colleague Letter and the 2024 DOJ Title II web rule (April 2026 deadline for large public institutions) frame enforcement. Common violations: inaccessible LMS, uncaptioned video, inaccessible proctoring software, image-only PDF course materials, inaccessible online testing platforms.
short_description_simple: Your college or university uses online tools — learning management systems (Canvas, Blackboard), video lectures, online tests, course PDFs — that don't work for people with disabilities. This is illegal under federal law. Report it to DOJ AND your school's disability services office.

case_name: Airline wheelchair damage, delay, or loss
eligibility:
The ACAA protects you if:
- You traveled (or attempted to travel) on a U.S. domestic flight, OR
- You traveled on any flight (US or foreign carrier) to or from the United States.
- You have a wheelchair, power chair, scooter, walker, CPAP, oxygen equipment, or other medically necessary mobility/medical device.
- The airline damaged, lost, or delayed return of your device.

You don't need a doctor's letter, a disability ID, or any special status. Using the device is enough.
eligibility_professional: ACAA coverage extends to all persons using mobility aids or medically necessary equipment on flights operated by U.S. carriers (domestic and international) and on foreign-carrier flights to and from the United States, 14 C.F.R. § 382.7. Coverage is not conditioned on prior medical-documentation submission to the carrier.
eligibility_simple:
You are covered if:
- You flew on a US airline, or any airline going to/from the US.
- You have a wheelchair, scooter, walker, CPAP, oxygen, or similar device.
- The airline broke, lost, or didn't return your device on time.

You don't need a doctor's note or any special form. You just need to use the device.
full_description:
Airlines damage, lose, or delay wheelchairs and other mobility aids thousands of times each year. The Air Carrier Access Act (ACAA) — a federal law separate from the ADA — and DOT regulations at 14 CFR Part 382 require airlines to stow your wheelchair safely; return your chair to you AT the gate, not at baggage claim, undamaged and assembled; repair or replace damaged chairs at the airline's expense; provide a comparable loaner chair while yours is being repaired; and have a Complaint Resolution Official (CRO) on duty at every airport to handle disputes in real time.
