Despite these rules, damage incidents are widespread. DOT publishes monthly statistics showing that mishandled wheelchair and scooter rates have remained stubbornly high across most major carriers.

You don't have a class action to join, but you have strong individual remedies: file a DOT complaint at airconsumer.dot.gov; demand a written incident report from the airline at the airport BEFORE you leave (ask for the CRO if needed); file a claim with the airline for repair, replacement, AND consequential damages; file a separate complaint with the airline's consumer affairs office; and if serious, contact disability-rights counsel.
full_description_professional:
The Air Carrier Access Act, 49 U.S.C. § 41705, and DOT implementing regulations at 14 C.F.R. Part 382 (the comprehensive Title III-analog framework for air carriers), govern airline handling of mobility aids. Substantive obligations include:

- § 382.81: Acceptance of wheelchairs and other mobility aids without limitation as cargo.
- § 382.93: Provision of accessible storage in the cabin where size permits.
- § 382.125: Return of mobility aids at the gate (jet-bridge return) of the destination airport.
- § 382.131: Liability for damage at full replacement value, without the Montreal Convention limits that apply to other baggage.
- § 382.151-§ 382.155: Complaint Resolution Official (CRO) requirement — every covered airport must have a CRO available during operating hours.

The 2024 DOT enhanced enforcement rule increased civil-penalty exposure for repeat or egregious mishandling. DOT publishes monthly Air Travel Consumer Reports including wheelchair-mishandling data; some carriers have publicly committed to enhanced protocols following enforcement actions.

Affected travelers should pursue parallel remedies:
- DOT administrative complaint at airconsumer.dot.gov, which triggers carrier investigation and may yield civil-penalty enforcement.
- On-site CRO engagement preserving the contemporaneous record.
- Direct claim against the carrier for replacement value (no Montreal Convention cap), consequential damages, and reasonable alternative-equipment provision.
- Where serious, individual ACAA litigation; the statute permits private rights of action in limited circumstances, but DOT and state consumer-protection enforcement are typically more efficient. Coordinate with disability-rights counsel (NDRN, Paralyzed Veterans of America, Disability Rights Advocates) for systemic pattern documentation.
full_description_simple:
Airlines hurt, lose, or delay thousands of wheelchairs every year.

The law is called the Air Carrier Access Act. It says airlines must:
- Be careful with your chair.
- Give your chair back to you at the gate when you get off — not at baggage claim.
- Fix or replace your chair if they break it.
- Give you a backup chair to use while yours is being fixed.
- Have a special manager at every airport (called a CRO) who handles wheelchair problems.

But airlines still break wheelchairs all the time.

If this happens to you:

DO RIGHT AWAY (at the airport):
- Ask for the CRO. Don't leave the airport without making them write down what happened.
- Take photos of the damage.
- Get every piece of paper they give you.

DO WITHIN A DAY OR TWO:
- File a complaint with the U.S. Department of Transportation at airconsumer.dot.gov. It's free.
- Call the airline's special needs office. Ask for repair, replacement, and reimbursement.
- If you missed work or had medical problems because of the damage, say so. You can ask for that money back too.

DO IF IT'S BAD:
- Tell a disability rights lawyer. There are lawyers who handle just these cases.

key_dates.Current DOT regulations: 14 C.F.R. Part 382 (substantially revised 2008, with subsequent updates)
key_dates.Status: Active enforcement; no current consumer class action; individual + DOT-administrative remedies

legal_theory: Air Carrier Access Act (49 USC § 41705) + 14 CFR Part 382
short_description: Mishandled, broken, or lost wheelchairs and mobility aids during air travel. No current consumer class action — file a DOT complaint, document, and share with us.
short_description_professional: Air Carrier Access Act (49 U.S.C. § 41705) and DOT regulations at 14 C.F.R. Part 382 govern wheelchair and mobility-aid handling by U.S.-based and foreign carriers in U.S. air travel. No current consumer class action; individual DOT complaints + carrier-direct claims are the operative paths. Damage incidents have ranged from minor frame damage to total destruction, with reported aggregate damage exceeding 11,000 mobility devices per year industry-wide.
short_description_simple: Your wheelchair, scooter, or mobility aid was broken, lost, or delayed by an airline. The federal Air Carrier Access Act protects you. Report it to the airline AND the Department of Transportation right away.

case_name: Hospital and medical office accessible exam table failures
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a mobility disability — wheelchair, scooter, walker, paralysis, amputation, severe arthritis, etc. (Other disabilities also covered for related issues like communication access.)
- You attempted to receive medical care at any provider — hospital, primary care, specialist, urgent care, dental, OB/GYN, imaging center, mental health, etc.
- You couldn't access the equipment, weren't offered transfer assistance, were examined inadequately, were told to go elsewhere, or were told to bring family for transfer.

This applies to public AND private medical providers, including those that take Medicare/Medicaid.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 seeking medical, dental, mental health, or related services at any healthcare provider subject to Title II, Title III, § 504, or § 1557 jurisdiction. The breadth of § 504 coverage through Medicare/Medicaid participation creates near-universal federal anti-discrimination jurisdiction over US healthcare providers.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or have another mobility disability.
- You went to a doctor, hospital, or clinic.
- They couldn't fit you with the right equipment, wouldn't help you transfer, or told you to go somewhere else.
full_description:
Medical providers — hospitals, primary care, specialists, imaging centers, urgent care, dental, mental health, OB/GYN, and others — frequently fail to provide accessible medical equipment or transfer assistance for patients with mobility disabilities. This results in patients being examined while still in their wheelchairs (medically inadequate), being denied imaging or procedures entirely, or being told to bring family members to help transfer.

The legal framework is robust: ADA Title III covers most private medical providers; Title II covers public hospitals and clinics; Section 504 covers all federally funded providers (nearly every provider, via Medicare/Medicaid); Section 1557 of the ACA adds healthcare-specific obligations; and DOJ's May 2024 Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule requires accessible MDE — exam tables, weight scales, mammography, X-ray, MRI/CT positioning equipment — with phased acquisition and use obligations.

Common violations: fixed-height exam tables; no staff trained on transfer assistance; "bring a family member" policies; inaccessible imaging equipment; denial of service or referral elsewhere; and communication failures with deaf patients.

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; HHS Office for Civil Rights (the primary healthcare enforcer) at hhs.gov/ocr; the state medical board; State Protection & Advocacy; and Disability Rights Advocates (dralegal.org).
full_description_professional:
Medical equipment accessibility is governed by ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for private providers; Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for public providers; Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794, 45 C.F.R. Part 84) for federally funded providers (nearly universal via Medicare/Medicaid); and Section 1557 of the ACA (42 U.S.C. § 18116, 45 C.F.R. Part 92).

DOJ's May 2024 final rule at 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(f) (Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule) implements the U.S. Access Board MDE Standards (36 C.F.R. Part 1195) with binding effect: covered entities must acquire MDE meeting the standards going forward and bring existing equipment into compliance on phased schedules. Equipment categories include exam tables, weight scales, mammography systems, radiography, MRI/CT positioning, and others.

Substantive obligations: (i) reasonable modifications to policies, practices, and procedures including transfer-assistance protocols; (ii) MDE acquisition meeting accessibility standards; (iii) staff training on transfer techniques and accommodation provision; (iv) effective-communication provision for deaf patients (28 C.F.R. § 36.303 + § 35.160); (v) no service denial or improper referral as a substitute for accommodation provision. The Fust v. First Urology pattern of class enforcement is now well-established.

Enforcement avenues: HHS OCR (primary healthcare enforcement; particularly responsive to § 1557 and § 504 claims); DOJ Civil Rights Division (Title III enforcement); state medical boards and licensing authorities; state P&A entities; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; private § 504 / § 1557 actions for individualized compensatory damages where deliberate indifference is established.
full_description_simple:
Doctors and hospitals have to make their exam tables and machines work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The exam table is too high — you can't get on it from your wheelchair.
- Staff says "we don't help with transfers."
- The mammogram machine doesn't work for someone who can't stand.
- The MRI tube can't be entered from a wheelchair.
- The doctor examines you while you're still in your chair (which is bad medical care).
- They tell you to go somewhere else, like a hospital.
- They tell you to bring a family member to help.

The law:
- The ADA covers private doctors and hospitals.
- Section 504 covers any healthcare provider that takes Medicare or Medicaid (almost all of them).
- A new federal rule (May 2024) makes specific requirements about exam tables, weight scales, mammograms, and other equipment.

If you're dealing with this, the same thing happened to the patients in the Fust v. First Urology case (you can see that one in our case list).

Where to report:
- HHS Office for Civil Rights at hhs.gov/ocr — they handle healthcare complaints.
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Disability Rights Advocates at dralegal.org.
- Your state medical board.

key_dates.Related settled case: Fust v. First Urology (3:20-cv-00562 W.D. Ky.)

legal_theory: ADA Title III + Section 504 + DOJ MDE Rule (2024)
short_description: Medical providers without accessible exam tables, diagnostic equipment, or transfer policies. DOJ MDE rule (May 2024) makes new compliance deadlines enforceable.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III + Section 504 + Section 1557 framework for medical-equipment accessibility. DOJ's May 2024 Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule (28 C.F.R. § 36.302(f)) establishes specific accessible-MDE acquisition and transfer-assistance obligations with phased compliance deadlines. Common targets: hospitals, primary care, specialist offices, imaging centers, urgent care, dental clinics.
short_description_simple: Your doctor's office, hospital, or clinic doesn't have an exam table or imaging machine you can actually use, OR staff refused to help you transfer from your wheelchair. This is illegal. A new federal rule (May 2024) makes the standards specific and enforceable.

case_name: United States investigation of FlixBus and Greyhound Lines
court: U.S. Department of Justice investigation (no court filed yet)
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on intercity bus riders with mobility, sensory, or other disabilities who use FlixBus or Greyhound.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You have a disability that affects travel — wheelchair use, mobility impairment, sensory disability, etc.
- You tried to take a FlixBus or Greyhound trip (or wanted to) in the past 2-3 years.
- You experienced barriers: the wheelchair lift didn't work or the driver refused to deploy it; you couldn't reserve accessible service; you were stranded at a terminal; you were charged extra for accessibility; or you experienced discrimination from drivers or staff.

This is investigation, not litigation.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who used or attempted to use FlixBus or Greyhound intercity bus service may submit experiences to support the federal investigation. Both DOJ Title III and FMCSA DOT-regulation enforcement avenues are available; § 1983 actions are not available against private carriers, but Title III private rights of action provide for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees.
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You have a disability that affects how you travel (wheelchair, walker, vision loss, etc.).
- You took (or tried to take) a FlixBus or Greyhound bus.
- Something went wrong with the lift, the driver, the reservation, or the bus itself.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. — two of the largest intercity bus carriers in the United States. FlixBus acquired Greyhound in 2021, consolidating the brands under a single corporate parent.

Intercity buses are covered by the ADA AND by Department of Transportation regulations at 49 C.F.R. Part 37 (Over-the-Road Bus Accessibility), which is a more demanding framework than Title III alone. The regulations require wheelchair lifts on every bus, 48-hour-advance reservation for accessible service, accessible reservation systems (phone and online), and trained drivers who can operate the lift and secure mobility devices.

DOJ is investigating allegations that FlixBus and Greyhound systemically fail to comply: wheelchair lifts that aren't maintained or operable; drivers who refuse to deploy the lift; reservation systems that don't let wheelchair users actually reserve accessible service; passengers left stranded at terminals when the lift doesn't work or the bus that arrives isn't the promised accessible bus.

The investigation is in active phase — not yet resolved by consent decree or litigation. Riders who've experienced these barriers should report.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division opened an investigation of FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and the Department of Transportation accessibility regulations at 49 C.F.R. Part 37 (specifically pt. 37 subpt. H for over-the-road buses). FlixBus acquired Greyhound in 2021, creating a consolidated intercity bus carrier subject to integrated enforcement.

DOT regulations impose stringent obligations on over-the-road bus carriers: 49 C.F.R. § 37.7 (entity-wide accessibility requirements); § 37.165 (lift-and-securement equipment on all vehicles); § 37.171 (driver training); § 37.209 (specific advance-reservation procedures for accessible service); § 37.211 (accessible-vehicle deployment for advance-reserved trips); § 37.215 (terminal accessibility).

DOJ is investigating allegations of systemic non-compliance: lift unavailability/inoperability; driver refusal of accommodation citing "lift broken" or "not trained"; reservation-system failures preventing wheelchair users from completing 48-hour advance accessible-service reservations; passenger stranding at terminals when scheduled accessible service is not provided; and inadequate complaint-handling and remediation.

The investigation is in active phase. Riders may report to DOJ, FMCSA (DOT enforcement, 1-800-FORCE-1), private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN), and state civil-rights agencies. Title III provides for injunctive relief and fees; DOT regulations may support administrative remediation.
full_description_simple:
DOJ is looking into two big intercity bus companies: FlixBus and Greyhound. (FlixBus bought Greyhound a few years ago, so they're really one company now.)

People who use wheelchairs say:
- The lifts on the buses don't work.
- Drivers say "the lift is broken" or "I can't use it" and leave wheelchair users at the bus station.
- It's hard to reserve an accessible seat 48 hours ahead like the rules say.
- The website and phone line don't handle accessible bookings well.

Intercity buses have stricter rules than other businesses. They have to have lifts. They have to have drivers who can use the lifts. They have to let you reserve accessible service.

If this has happened to you, you can:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Call DOJ Disability Rights Section: 800-514-0301.
- File with the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (DOT's bus regulator).
- Report through your state attorney general.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement; FMCSA administrative action also possible
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

legal_theory: ADA Title III + 49 CFR Part 37 (over-the-road bus)
short_description: DOJ investigating intercity bus carriers over wheelchair accommodation failures and inaccessible booking. Share your experience.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title III and 49 C.F.R. Part 37 of FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. (intercity over-the-road bus carriers). Investigation addresses wheelchair-lift availability and operability, drivers' refusal of accommodation, accessible-booking system failures, and over-the-road bus accessibility deficiencies under DOT regulations.
short_description_simple: DOJ is investigating FlixBus and Greyhound. Wheelchair users say the buses don't have working lifts, drivers refuse to help, and the booking system is hard to use. If this happened to you, tell DOJ.

case_name: United States v. Sea Mar Community Health Centers
court: U.S. Department of Justice administrative settlement
eligibility:
The settlement protects patients with disabilities at Sea Mar Community Health Centers in Washington state.

You can report a problem if:
- You're a Sea Mar patient (current or past).
- You have a disability.
- You encountered a barrier: couldn't access the building or exam room, no ASL interpreter provided when needed, no accessible dental chair available, communication accommodations refused, etc.

This is Sea Mar-specific. The same federal framework (ADA Title III + Section 504 + Section 1557 for FQHCs) applies to other providers.
eligibility_professional: Patients with qualifying disabilities (42 U.S.C. § 12102) seeking services at any Sea Mar facility in Washington state retain ongoing rights under the settlement. As an FQHC, Sea Mar is also bound by Section 504 (federal financial assistance trigger) and Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act for healthcare-specific anti-discrimination obligations.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You go (or went) to Sea Mar for dental or medical care.
- You have a disability.
- You had trouble getting accessible care — no interpreter, can't fit in the exam room with your wheelchair, no accessible dental chair, etc.

This is for Sea Mar only. But other clinics have similar rules.
full_description:
This is a 2025 DOJ enforcement settlement with Sea Mar Community Health Centers, a federally qualified health center (FQHC) network operating across Washington state.

DOJ found accessibility failures at Sea Mar's dental and medical clinics: wheelchair users couldn't access exam rooms or treatment areas; deaf patients weren't consistently provided sign-language interpreters or other auxiliary aids; accessible dental chairs and equipment weren't available or weren't maintained; communication accommodations weren't consistently offered.

The settlement requires Sea Mar to audit and remediate physical access at all locations; provide qualified ASL interpreters for deaf patients at no charge; procure and maintain accessible dental and medical equipment; train staff; and provide ongoing reporting to DOJ.

The case is in compliance phase. If you're a Sea Mar patient (current or past) and you've encountered accessibility barriers, you can report it.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Sea Mar Community Health Centers (DOJ administrative settlement, April 2025), is a settled enforcement action under Title III ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing accessibility deficiencies at Sea Mar's Washington-state dental and medical clinics. Sea Mar is a federally qualified health center (FQHC), implicating § 504 obligations beyond Title III alone.

DOJ findings: physical-access barriers; inconsistent provision of qualified ASL interpreters and auxiliary aids; absence of accessible dental and medical equipment; staff-training failures.

Settlement obligations: facility audit and remediation under ADA Standards (28 C.F.R. § 36.406 / 2010 Standards); auxiliary-aids provision per § 36.303 and § 504 effective-communication requirements; equipment procurement (height-adjustable exam tables, wheelchair-accessible dental chairs, patient lifts where indicated); staff training; DOJ monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Sea Mar patients encountering current violations should report to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), HHS Office for Civil Rights (Section 504/Section 1557 enforcement, particularly relevant for FQHCs), Disability Rights Washington, and Washington State Human Rights Commission.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Sea Mar Community Health Centers from April 2025.

Sea Mar runs many community health centers across Washington state. The DOJ found that Sea Mar was not making its dental and medical clinics accessible. Wheelchair users couldn't get into exam rooms. Deaf patients didn't get sign language interpreters when they needed them.

Sea Mar agreed to:
- Fix the buildings.
- Provide free ASL interpreters for deaf patients.
- Buy accessible dental chairs and equipment.
- Train staff.

If you go to Sea Mar and you have a disability, and they're not following these rules, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

short_description: April 2025 DOJ settlement over dental clinic accessibility. Report violations.
short_description_professional: April 2025 DOJ enforcement settlement under ADA Title III and Section 504 binding Sea Mar Community Health Centers (WA) to dental and medical-facility accessibility reforms. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Sea Mar from April 2025. Sea Mar (community health centers in Washington state) had to fix dental clinic accessibility — wheelchair access, communication for deaf patients, accessible exam chairs. If you've had problems at Sea Mar, you can report it.

case_name: United States investigation of Alaska Division of Elections
court: U.S. Department of Justice investigation (no court filed yet)
eligibility:
DOJ's investigation covers voters with disabilities in Alaska — anyone affected by polling-place, voting-equipment, or mail-ballot accessibility failures.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You're a registered voter in Alaska.
- You have any disability — mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, mental health, or other.
- You encountered any voting-access barrier: couldn't enter a polling place, couldn't use a voting machine, couldn't mark a mail ballot independently, were refused curbside voting, were refused poll-worker assistance, or were otherwise denied accommodation.

This is investigation, not litigation — there's no class to certify or join. But your story strengthens DOJ's evidence base.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 registered to vote in Alaska may submit experiences to support the federal investigation. No class certification or formal joinder is involved; reports constitute evidentiary contributions to the ongoing federal record.
eligibility_simple:
You can help if:
- You vote in Alaska.
- You have a disability.
- You've had any kind of voting problem because of a disability — at a polling place, with the voting machines, with your mail ballot, or anywhere else.

There's no class to join. DOJ just wants to hear your story.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the Alaska Division of Elections, opened in 2024.

In a June 2024 findings letter, DOJ documented that Alaska's polling places — including those used for state and federal elections — have widespread accessibility problems. Wheelchair users can't reach entrances. Blind voters can't use the equipment provided. Polling places lack accessible parking, accessible paths of travel, ramps, or accessible voting booths. The state's ballot delivery systems for voters with print disabilities don't meet ADA Title II and Help America Vote Act (HAVA) standards.

DOJ is now in active investigation phase. Unlike a settled consent decree, this case has NOT yet been resolved — the federal government is gathering evidence, may file a lawsuit if Alaska doesn't come to terms, and is actively seeking accounts from affected voters.

If you have a disability and you've had trouble voting in Alaska, your experience can support DOJ's investigation. Report polling-place physical barriers, inaccessible voting equipment, mail-ballot inaccessibility, curbside voting refusals, or any disability-related denial of accommodation.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division, in coordination with the Voting Section, opened an investigation of the Alaska Division of Elections in 2024 under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq.; the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081; and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794.

A June 2024 findings letter documents systemic deficiencies: polling-place physical access failures under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and ADA Standards (entrances, parking, paths of travel, accessible voting booths); accessible-voting-equipment deployment and operational failures (BMD/accessible-equipment availability, functionality, and poll-worker training); inaccessible ballot delivery to print-disabled voters; inadequate curbside voting alternatives.

The investigation remains in active phase — not yet resolved by consent decree or litigation. Voters with disabilities encountering barriers should report contemporaneously to support the evidence record. DOJ may pursue formal litigation under § 12133 absent voluntary compliance.

Reports should be filed with: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (ADA.gov; voting.section@usdoj.gov for civil-rights-specific intake); Alaska Division of Elections (administrative record); Disability Law Center of Alaska (state Protection & Advocacy entity); Alaska Native Civil Rights organizations where applicable for tribal-affiliated voters.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Alaska right now. The case is not over yet — they're still gathering information.

DOJ said in 2024 that Alaska has serious problems with voting. Many polling places have no wheelchair access. The voting machines don't work for blind voters. Mail ballots come on paper that print-disabled voters can't read on their own.

You can help. If you have a disability and you've had trouble voting in Alaska — at a polling place, voting by mail, or in any way — tell DOJ. They use real stories from voters to build their case.

You can:
- File a free complaint at ADA.gov.
- Tell the Disability Law Center of Alaska.
- File a complaint with the Alaska Division of Elections.

What to report:
- Polling places you couldn't enter.
- Voting machines that didn't work for you.
- Mail ballots you couldn't fill out on your own.
- Times you were refused curbside voting.
- Any time someone said you couldn't get an accommodation.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement litigation under 42 U.S.C. § 12133
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

short_description: DOJ findings letter alleges Alaska's polling places and ballot systems are inaccessible.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II, HAVA, and Section 504 of Alaska Division of Elections. June 2024 findings letter alleges systemic polling-place accessibility deficiencies and inaccessible ballot/voting-system infrastructure. Investigation in active evidence-gathering phase.
short_description_simple: The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Alaska right now. DOJ says many polling places aren't accessible and the voting system doesn't work for people with disabilities. If you've had trouble voting in Alaska, share your story — it helps the investigation.

case_name: United States investigation of State of Idaho — Olmstead Integration
court: U.S. Department of Justice investigation (no court filed yet)
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on Idaho residents with disabilities who are institutionalized or at serious risk of institutionalization.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You (or a family member) are currently in an Idaho nursing facility, ICF/IID, state psychiatric hospital, or similar institutional setting.
- You want to leave the institution but can't get sufficient community-based services to make that possible.
- You're on a Medicaid HCBS waiver waitlist.
- You were discharged from a hospital to a nursing facility when community placement would have been appropriate.
- You were discouraged or actively diverted from community placement.

Family members, advocates, and guardians can submit on behalf of the affected person.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who are currently institutionalized in Idaho nursing facilities, ICF/IIDs, or state psychiatric facilities, or are at serious risk of institutionalization due to inadequate community-based service capacity, may submit experiences supporting the federal Olmstead investigation. Family, guardians, and advocates may submit on the affected person's behalf.
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You (or your family member) is in an Idaho nursing home, ICF, state hospital, or institution.
- You (or they) want to leave but can't get enough services at home.
- You're on a waiting list for home-based services.
- You went from the hospital to a nursing home and feel like they didn't even try to send you home with help.

Family members can speak for the person.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the State of Idaho, opened with a January 2025 findings letter. The case is about Idaho's system for serving people with disabilities — specifically, people who could live in their own homes or in community-based settings but are instead placed in or stuck in nursing facilities.

The case is grounded in Olmstead v. L.C., a landmark 1999 U.S. Supreme Court decision interpreting the ADA. Olmstead held that unjustified institutionalization of people with disabilities — when community-based services would be appropriate — is illegal disability discrimination.

DOJ alleges that Idaho's system fails Olmstead: inadequate Medicaid home- and community-based services (HCBS) with long waitlists and insufficient capacity; hospital and nursing-facility discharge planning that defaults to long-term institutional placement; insufficient supports for people with intellectual and developmental disabilities, serious mental illness, and complex medical needs; and a funding and policy bias that makes institutionalization the path of least resistance.

The investigation is in active phase. Olmstead-grounded DOJ investigations have led to major statewide reform settlements in many states. If you (or a family member) is currently institutionalized in Idaho or has been on a waitlist for community services, your story supports the investigation.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division issued a findings letter in January 2025 alleging that the State of Idaho violates Title II of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, as interpreted in Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999), through unnecessary segregation of persons with disabilities in nursing facilities, intermediate care facilities for individuals with intellectual disabilities (ICF/IID), and state psychiatric facilities, when community-based services would be both appropriate and preferred.

The investigation framework follows the established DOJ Olmstead enforcement template — building on settlements with Connecticut, Georgia (DOJ v. State of Georgia, 2010), Mississippi (United States v. State of Mississippi, 2021), North Carolina, and Virginia — examining: (i) Medicaid HCBS waiver capacity and waitlists under 42 C.F.R. Part 441 Subpart G; (ii) hospital and SNF discharge-planning practices and defaults; (iii) crisis-services and pre-institutional-placement diversion capacity; (iv) state administrative and budgetary biases favoring institutional over community-based services; (v) inadequate transition supports for currently-institutionalized persons seeking community placement.

The investigation is in active phase. Resolution path involves either voluntary settlement (typical pattern: 5-10 year reform commitments with HCBS expansion targets, waitlist reduction, and quality monitoring) or DOJ enforcement litigation under 42 U.S.C. § 12133.

Stakeholders include: DisAbility Rights Idaho (state P&A); the National Disability Rights Network; CMS (federal Medicaid oversight); Idaho Department of Health and Welfare; local Centers for Independent Living. Reports to DOJ may proceed via ADA.gov; coordinate with DRI for systemic pattern documentation and individual transition advocacy.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is looking into the State of Idaho. They opened the investigation in January 2025.

DOJ says Idaho is sending people with disabilities to nursing homes when they could live at home or in their own community with help.

This is illegal. A Supreme Court case called Olmstead (from 1999) said that states can't put people with disabilities in nursing homes or institutions if those people could live in the community with the right help. It's a form of disability discrimination.

DOJ thinks Idaho:
- Doesn't have enough home-care services to actually let people live at home.
- Has waitlists that are too long for community services.
- Sends people from the hospital straight to nursing homes instead of helping them go home.
- Puts people with intellectual disabilities, serious mental illness, or complicated medical needs in institutions when they could be in the community.

If you (or your family member) is in an Idaho nursing home, hospital, or institution and wants to leave — or has been on a waitlist for home services — you can tell DOJ. Your story helps.

Where to share:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- DisAbility Rights Idaho.
- A Medicaid waiver coordinator.

key_dates.Precedent: Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999); DOJ settlements with GA (2010), MS (2021), NC, VA, CT
key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement (typical: 5-10 year reform commitment) or DOJ enforcement litigation
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

short_description: DOJ findings letter alleges Idaho needlessly segregates people with disabilities in nursing facilities instead of community-based services. Share your experience to support the investigation.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II and Section 504 of State of Idaho, addressing unnecessary segregation of persons with disabilities in nursing facilities in violation of Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999). January 2025 findings letter alleges Idaho's service system funnels persons with disabilities into institutional placements when community-based alternatives would be appropriate and preferred.
short_description_simple: DOJ is investigating Idaho. DOJ says Idaho is keeping people with disabilities in nursing homes when they could live in their own homes or communities with help. This violates a Supreme Court case called Olmstead. If you or a family member is stuck in an Idaho nursing facility, share your story.

case_name: United States v. Uber Technologies, Inc.
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
eligibility:
You can support DOJ's active case (and pursue your own remedies) if:
- You have a disability — service-animal use, wheelchair use (including stowable wheelchairs), or other disability covered by Title III.
- You used or tried to use Uber.
- A driver refused you, charged you improperly, or otherwise discriminated against you because of disability.

Reports of incidents from any state are useful for DOJ's case. State-law remedies are stronger in CA, NY, MA, and other states with statutory-damages frameworks.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who used or attempted to use Uber and encountered driver-level discrimination or improper surcharge assessment. Reports support DOJ's federal enforcement record; parallel state-law and private-remedy avenues remain available.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability.
- An Uber driver refused you or charged you improperly because of your disability.

DOJ wants to hear from you. Your story helps the case.
full_description:
On September 11, 2025, the U.S. Department of Justice sued Uber Technologies, Inc. under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act. The case is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California (3:25-cv-07731-SK).

DOJ alleges that Uber routinely denies rides to passengers with service animals (often guide dogs); routinely denies rides to passengers with stowable wheelchairs that can fit in standard Uber vehicles; charges improper surcharges and cleaning fees related to disability; and has failed to adequately train drivers and enforce its own anti-discrimination policies.

This is a NEW case — separate from the 2016 NFB-CA v. Uber settlement (still in compliance phase). DOJ's case applies the federal government's direct enforcement authority to Uber's ongoing pattern of driver-level discrimination.

If you've had an Uber driver refuse you because of your service animal, refuse to stow your wheelchair, or charge you a disability-related fee, you can support DOJ's case by reporting the incident. You can also report through Uber's in-app system and via DOJ at ADA.gov. Litigation may take years.

Where to report: DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov; civilrights.justice.gov; Uber's in-app reporting; NFB testing program at testing@nfb.org; for California residents, the California Civil Rights Department for Unruh Act statutory damages.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 3:25-cv-07731-SK (N.D. Cal., filed Sept. 11, 2025), is an active DOJ Civil Rights Division enforcement action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq.

The complaint alleges systemic Title III violations: (i) driver-level denial of service to passengers with service animals as defined under 28 C.F.R. § 36.104 (impermissible defenses including allergies, fear, and religious objection per § 36.302(c)(2)); (ii) denial of service to passengers with stowable wheelchairs (failing § 36.302(a) reasonable-modification obligations); (iii) imposition of surcharges and cleaning fees prohibited by § 36.301(c); (iv) inadequate driver training and policy enforcement.

The action parallels but is distinct from NFB-CA v. Uber, 3:14-cv-04086 (settled 2016, ongoing compliance phase — see related listing). NFB-CA v. Uber created private-settlement enforcement mechanisms (in-app reporting, NFB testing, judicial monitor). The DOJ case invokes federal direct-enforcement authority and may yield compensatory damages, civil penalties, and additional injunctive relief beyond the 2016 settlement.

Disposition timeline: pleadings, discovery, and motion practice are ongoing as of mid-2026. Settlement is possible at any stage; trial likely 2027 or later if not settled.

Active enforcement avenues for affected passengers: DOJ complaint at ADA.gov and civilrights.justice.gov; Uber in-app reporting (settlement-required); NFB testing program; state AG and state civil-rights agencies for statutory-damages jurisdictions (CA Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA); private Title III + state-law counsel for individual claims.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice sued Uber in September 2025. DOJ is the federal government's top civil rights enforcer.

DOJ says Uber drivers:
- Refuse to give rides to blind people with guide dogs.
- Refuse to stow wheelchairs that easily fit in the car.
- Charge extra fees because of disability.

This is a new case. There's already an older settlement from 2016 (NFB-CA v. Uber, see related listing). Both are still active — the new DOJ case adds to the pressure on Uber.

What you should do if a driver refuses you:
- Report in the Uber app right away.
- File a DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Tell the National Federation of the Blind testing program.
- For California: file with the CA Civil Rights Department — you can get $4,000 per incident under state law.
- For other states with strong laws (NY, MA, etc.): file with your state attorney general.

key_dates.Status: Active litigation; early phase

short_description: DOJ alleges Uber and its drivers routinely deny rides to passengers with service animals and stowable wheelchairs, and charge improper surcharges.
short_description_professional: United States v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 3:25-cv-07731-SK (N.D. Cal., filed Sept. 11, 2025). Active DOJ enforcement under Title III ADA alleging systemic driver-level discrimination against passengers with service animals and stowable wheelchairs, plus improper surcharge assessment. Parallel to and supplementing the 2016 NFB-CA v. Uber settlement (3:14-cv-04086).
short_description_simple: The U.S. Department of Justice sued Uber in September 2025. DOJ says Uber drivers refuse rides to people with service animals and wheelchairs, and charge extra fees they shouldn't. This is a new federal case, separate from the 2016 settlement.

case_name: United States v. Los Angeles County
court: U.S. Department of Justice administrative settlement
eligibility:
The settlement protects voters with disabilities encountering accessibility barriers at LA County vote centers.

You can report a problem if:
- You're a registered voter in LA County.
- You have any disability — mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, etc.
- You encountered a barrier when voting at an LA County vote center: couldn't enter the building, couldn't access the parking, accessible voting machine wasn't working or wasn't set up, curbside voting wasn't offered or was inadequate, poll workers were unhelpful or untrained.

This is LA County-specific. Other California counties have their own obligations.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities registered to vote in Los Angeles County who encounter Title II/HAVA-implicating barriers at vote centers retain reporting and enforcement rights under the settlement and concurrent federal/state law.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You vote in LA County.
- You have a disability.
- You had trouble voting at a vote center — couldn't get in, the machine wasn't working, no one offered you curbside voting, etc.

This is for LA County only. Other counties in California have their own rules.
full_description:
This is a 2024 DOJ settlement with Los Angeles County over accessibility at the County's vote centers — the polling locations LA County uses in lieu of traditional precinct polling places.

DOJ found that LA County's vote centers had pervasive accessibility barriers: inaccessible building entrances, lack of accessible parking, voting equipment that wasn't set up for blind voters or voters with mobility disabilities, and inadequate curbside-voting alternatives for voters who couldn't safely enter the building.

The settlement requires LA County to audit and remediate physical access barriers at all vote-center facilities; ensure accessible voting machines are deployed and functioning at every vote center; provide curbside voting at every vote center; train poll workers on accessibility obligations; and submit to ongoing DOJ monitoring and election-cycle reporting.

The case is in compliance phase. If you're a voter with a disability in LA County and you've encountered barriers at a vote center, you can report it.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Los Angeles County (DOJ administrative settlement, August 2024), is a settled enforcement action under Title II ADA and the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081, addressing physical and programmatic accessibility deficiencies at LA County vote centers.

DOJ findings: pervasive physical-access barriers (entrances, parking, paths of travel under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and ADA Standards); accessible-voting-machine deployment and operational failures (BMD/accessible-equipment availability and functionality); inadequate or unoffered curbside-voting alternatives despite statutory mandate.

Settlement obligations: facility-level remediation per audit; consistent accessible-equipment deployment; mandated curbside-voting availability at all centers; poll-worker training; election-cycle reporting; DOJ monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Voters with disabilities encountering current barriers should report to DOJ Civil Rights Division (Voting Section, ADA.gov), LA County Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk, California Civil Rights Department, and disability-rights advocacy organizations.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and LA County from 2024.

LA County uses "vote centers" instead of regular polling places. The DOJ found that many vote centers were not accessible. Wheelchair users couldn't get in. Blind voters couldn't use the voting machines. Curbside voting (where someone brings the ballot to your car) was not really offered, even when the law said it should be.

LA County had to:
- Fix the buildings.
- Make sure accessible voting machines work at every vote center.
- Offer curbside voting at every vote center.
- Train poll workers.
- Let DOJ check up on them after each election.

If you have a disability and had trouble voting at an LA County vote center, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase; election-cycle reporting ongoing

short_description: DOJ settlement August 2024 requires LA County to provide accessible vote centers and curbside voting. Report violations.
short_description_professional: August 2024 DOJ settlement with Los Angeles County under ADA Title II and HAVA addressing vote-center accessibility deficiencies and mandating curbside voting availability for voters with disabilities. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with LA County from 2024. LA County has to make sure voters with disabilities can use vote centers — including curbside voting if they can't get inside. If you've had problems voting in LA County because of accessibility, you can report it.

case_name: United States investigation of Arizona Department of Child Safety
court: U.S. Department of Justice investigation (no court filed yet)
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on Arizona parents with disabilities subjected to DCS proceedings.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You're a parent (current or former) with any disability — physical, intellectual, mental health, sensory, cognitive, or other.
- You've had any DCS involvement: an open investigation, a removal, a hearing, a reunification process, or termination of parental rights.
- You believe DCS treated you differently because of your disability OR failed to provide an accommodation you needed.

This is investigation, not litigation — there's no class to join. Caregivers, grandparents, foster parents, kinship caregivers, and other family members can also report on the parent's behalf.
eligibility_professional:
Parents with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who have been subjected to AZ DCS investigations, dependency proceedings, removal proceedings, reunification services, or termination of parental rights may submit experiences to support the federal investigation.

Standing for individualized civil-rights claims may also exist under § 1983 (Equal Protection, Due Process), 42 U.S.C. § 12132, and 29 U.S.C. § 794. Such claims are subject to statutes of limitations (generally 2 years in Arizona for federal civil-rights claims).
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You're a parent in Arizona.
- You have a disability of any kind.
- DCS has been involved with your family — they investigated, they took your child, you had hearings, you had reunification services, etc.
- You think DCS treated you unfairly because of your disability, or didn't give you the help you needed.

Family members can also share the story on behalf of a parent.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the Arizona Department of Child Safety (DCS), opened in December 2024.

DOJ is investigating allegations that DCS systematically discriminates against parents with disabilities — physical, intellectual, mental health, and other — in child welfare proceedings. The investigation focuses on DCS investigation practices that treat disability as evidence of unfitness without proper assessment; failures to provide reasonable accommodations during investigations, hearings, and family-reunification services; communication failures with parents who are deaf, blind, or have cognitive disabilities; use of disability status as grounds for removal or termination of parental rights when the actual safety concern doesn't require removal; and inadequate adaptive parenting services and supports.

This pattern — disability discrimination in child welfare — has been the subject of major DOJ enforcement against multiple states (most notably Massachusetts in 2015). Arizona is the current focus.

The investigation is in active phase. Parents with disabilities who have had children removed or have experienced DCS proceedings without accommodation should report their experiences. This is not a class action.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division opened an investigation of the Arizona Department of Child Safety in December 2024 under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing alleged systemic disability discrimination in child welfare proceedings.

The investigation framework — building on the 2015 Massachusetts DCF settlement and analogous matters — examines: (i) the use of disability status as proxy evidence of parental unfitness without individualized functional assessment; (ii) failures to provide reasonable accommodations (qualified interpreters, accessible documents, communication accommodations) during DCS investigations, dependency proceedings, and reunification services; (iii) inadequate adaptive parenting services and assistive technology supports; (iv) communication barriers with parents who are deaf, blind, or have cognitive disabilities; (v) over-reliance on removal where less-restrictive supports would address safety concerns.

The investigation remains in active phase — no consent decree or litigation yet. Parents with disabilities subjected to DCS proceedings should report contemporaneously to support the federal evidence record.

Reports may proceed via: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov); Arizona Center for Disability Law (state Protection & Advocacy entity); The Arc of Arizona; private family/dependency-law counsel; § 1983 actions for specific civil-rights violations may proceed in parallel.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is looking into Arizona's child welfare agency. They opened the investigation in December 2024.

DOJ thinks that Arizona DCS is treating parents unfairly because of their disabilities. They are taking children away from parents who have disabilities — like physical disabilities, mental health conditions, intellectual disabilities, or being deaf or blind — without giving parents the help they need to parent safely.

Examples of what DOJ is looking at:
- Sign language interpreters not provided to deaf parents at meetings or hearings.
- Reading materials given to parents with intellectual disabilities in formats they can't read.
- DCS deciding a parent isn't fit just because of a mental health condition, without looking at what they actually do.
- Children removed when actually the parent just needed support, not separation.

If this has happened to you or your family in Arizona, you can tell DOJ. They use real stories to build their case.

Where to tell your story:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free, online).
- The Arizona Center for Disability Law (the state P&A).
- The Arc of Arizona.
- A family lawyer if you have one.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement litigation
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet
