short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II and Section 504 of Arizona Department of Child Safety. December 2024 investigation opened over alleged discrimination against parents with disabilities in child welfare proceedings, including failures to provide reasonable accommodations during investigations, hearings, and reunification services.
short_description_simple: The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Arizona's Department of Child Safety. DOJ thinks DCS is taking children from parents with disabilities without proper accommodations. If this has happened to you in Arizona, share your story.

case_name: United States v. City of Anoka, Minnesota
court: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota
eligibility:
The settlement directly binds the City of Anoka, MN. It does not create class membership for residents of other municipalities.

You can report a problem under this settlement if:
- You were a tenant in Anoka.
- You were threatened with eviction, evicted, or had your landlord pressured by Anoka's crime-free housing program.
- The police-contact incident(s) were related to a mental health disability — yours, a family member's, or another household resident's.

For residents of other cities with crime-free housing programs: this case is precedent. You can use it to support a DOJ complaint about your own city under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
eligibility_professional: Direct settlement coverage extends to tenants in Anoka, MN affected by the crime-free housing ordinance's pre-2024 enforcement. Persons in analogous jurisdictions may invoke the settlement as supportive precedent in DOJ administrative complaints and § 1983 actions challenging similar programs.
eligibility_simple:
You can report under THIS specific settlement if:
- You lived in Anoka, Minnesota as a tenant.
- Your landlord tried to evict you (or did evict you) because police were called.
- The police calls were because of a mental health crisis.

If you live in a DIFFERENT city with a similar "crime-free housing" program, this case is still useful — you can use it to support your own DOJ complaint. Many cities have these programs.
full_description:
This is a landmark 2024 DOJ enforcement settlement with the City of Anoka, Minnesota. The case addresses a category of municipal program — "crime-free housing" — that exists in hundreds of U.S. cities. These programs require landlords to evict tenants over interactions with police, including calls for help during a mental health crisis.

DOJ found that Anoka's crime-free housing program, as implemented, illegally targeted tenants with mental health disabilities. People calling 911 during psychiatric crises, family members calling for welfare checks, or any interaction with police related to disability-driven behavior triggered eviction proceedings. The result: tenants with serious mental illness lost their housing precisely when they most needed it.

The settlement requires Anoka to end the practice of counting mental-health-related police contacts as "criminal activity" for purposes of the ordinance; provide reasonable accommodations to tenants who interact with police due to a disability; train landlords and police on disability-rights obligations; audit prior enforcement and remediate evictions that violated the settlement standards; and submit to DOJ monitoring.

The case sets enforcement precedent for similar crime-free housing programs nationwide.
full_description_professional:
United States v. City of Anoka, Minnesota (D. Minn., 2024 settlement), is a landmark DOJ enforcement action under Title II ADA addressing the disability-discriminatory enforcement of municipal "crime-free housing" ordinances. The case targets a category of municipal program existing in hundreds of jurisdictions that conditions landlord licenses on tenant-eviction obligations triggered by police-contact thresholds.

DOJ found that the program's enforcement counted mental-health-disability-related police contacts (911 wellness calls, psychiatric-crisis welfare checks, behavior driven by qualifying psychiatric conditions) as "criminal activity" for ordinance purposes, resulting in eviction threats and removals targeting tenants with serious mental illness. The settlement implements 28 C.F.R. § 35.130 (prohibitions on disparate treatment), § 35.130(b)(7) (reasonable modifications), and § 35.130(b)(8) (eligibility criteria).

Settlement obligations: ordinance and policy revision excluding mental-health-related police contacts from "criminal activity" definitions; reasonable-accommodation pathway for tenants whose police contacts arise from qualifying disabilities; affirmative-training mandates for municipal staff and licensed landlords; retrospective audit and remediation of prior enforcement; DOJ monitoring.

The settlement establishes enforcement template for analogous claims against other municipalities. Affected tenants in other crime-free-housing jurisdictions should pursue DOJ administrative complaints and may invoke this settlement as supportive precedent.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and the City of Anoka, Minnesota from 2024.

Anoka had a program called "crime-free housing." It told landlords they had to kick out tenants if the police got called to their apartment too many times. Even if the call was someone asking for help during a mental health crisis. Even if the tenant was the one calling 911 for help.

The DOJ said this was illegal. It was punishing people for being sick.

Anoka had to:
- Stop counting calls for mental-health help as "crime."
- Help tenants stay in their homes when police are called for disability reasons.
- Train landlords and police about disability rights.
- Fix evictions that already happened because of this.

Many other cities have programs like this. If you or someone you know was pushed out of an apartment because the police were called when they were having a mental health crisis, you can report it.

key_dates.Precedent value: Template for analogous claims against other municipalities
key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

short_description: Landmark 2025 DOJ agreement addressing crime-free housing program discrimination against tenants with mental health disabilities. Report violations elsewhere using same model.
short_description_professional: 2024 DOJ enforcement settlement against City of Anoka, MN, under ADA Title II addressing crime-free housing ordinance enforcement targeting tenants with mental health and behavioral disabilities. Compliance phase; precedent template for analogous enforcement against other municipalities operating crime-free housing programs.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with the City of Anoka, Minnesota from 2024. Anoka had a "crime-free housing" program that pushed people with mental health disabilities out of their apartments. The DOJ said this is illegal disability discrimination and Anoka had to stop. If your city has a similar program, you can use this case as a precedent to report it.

case_name: Harris v. Union Pacific Railroad Co. (class action — decertified)
court: U.S. District Court, District of Nebraska
short_description: Class action challenging Union Pacific's 1% Rule fitness-for-duty policy. Class certified Feb 2019, decertified by Eighth Circuit March 2020 for lack of commonality. Class action closed Nov 2021; the underlying ADA Title I theory continues to be litigated through dozens of individual actions (Sanders, DeFries, Donahue, Zaragoza, Mlsna).

case_name: Liberty Resources, Inc. v. City of Philadelphia
court: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Pennsylvania
eligibility:
The settlement protects people with mobility disabilities in Philadelphia and visitors who experience curb-ramp barriers anywhere in the city.

You can report a problem if:
- You use a wheelchair, walker, scooter, cane, or other mobility device.
- You encountered a Philadelphia city sidewalk where a curb ramp was missing, broken, too steep, or otherwise inaccessible.
- The barrier kept you from crossing the street, or forced you into the road, or stranded you on a block.

This case is about Philadelphia City sidewalks. It does NOT cover SEPTA or transit-related accessibility, private property, or sidewalks in suburban townships.
eligibility_professional:
The settlement creates ongoing rights for persons with qualifying mobility disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who encounter inaccessible pedestrian rights-of-way maintained by the City of Philadelphia.

Scope limitations:
- City-maintained pedestrian rights-of-way only. SEPTA-owned or -maintained facilities are subject to separate Title II claims.
- Excludes private-property pedestrian features. State and federal property within Philadelphia (federal courthouses, state buildings) are subject to separate Title II/Section 504 enforcement.

Reporting and enforcement:
- Plaintiffs' counsel (Liberty Resources): contact for systemic violation reporting.
- Philadelphia Department of Streets: city-level complaint process.
- DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
- Direct settlement enforcement in E.D. Pa. for substantial systemic non-compliance.
eligibility_simple:
You can report a problem in Philadelphia if:
- You use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or other mobility device.
- You ran into a Philadelphia city sidewalk where the curb ramp was missing, broken, or too steep.
- The problem kept you from crossing safely.

It does NOT cover:
- SEPTA buses, trains, or stations.
- Sidewalks in townships outside Philadelphia.
- Private property sidewalks.
full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of Philadelphia, filed by Liberty Resources, Inc. (a Philadelphia center for independent living) in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania in August 2019.

The lawsuit challenged Philadelphia's decades-long failure to install ADA-compliant curb ramps at intersections and to maintain existing ramps when they fell into disrepair. People who use wheelchairs, walkers, scooters, or have other mobility disabilities were effectively cut off from the public sidewalk network — unable to cross streets safely, forced into the roadway with vehicle traffic, or stuck on a block with no way out.

The case was settled and Philadelphia agreed to a remediation schedule covering the entire city, with priority routes, deadlines, and reporting obligations to plaintiffs and the court. The settlement is in compliance phase.

If you encounter a missing curb ramp, a curb ramp that doesn't meet ADA Standards (too steep, no detectable warning, no flush transition, located out of the line of travel), or a damaged ramp that should have been repaired, you can report it to Liberty Resources or directly to Philadelphia.
full_description_professional:
Liberty Resources, Inc. v. City of Philadelphia (E.D. Pa., filed Aug. 26, 2019), is a settled class action under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing the City's programmatic failure to provide accessible curb ramps and maintain them in compliance with ADA Standards for Accessible Design (§§ 4.7 / 406, depending on applicable design epoch) and 28 C.F.R. § 35.151(i).

The settlement binds the City to a court-supervised remediation schedule covering installation of new ramps at uncompleted intersections and replacement/repair of non-compliant or deteriorated ramps. The schedule incorporates priority routing, periodic reporting obligations, and an ongoing private right to enforce compliance.

The matter remains in compliance phase. Reports of non-compliance — including missing ramps, ramps failing applicable slope/transition/detectable-warning requirements, mislocated ramps not aligned with the pedestrian path of travel, or ramps left unrepaired beyond scheduled maintenance windows — may be made to plaintiffs' counsel (Liberty Resources) and to the Philadelphia Department of Streets. DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170 are also available.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against the City of Philadelphia. It is now settled.

A group called Liberty Resources sued the city in 2019. They said Philadelphia has not put curb ramps at most street corners. Without ramps, people who use wheelchairs, walkers, or scooters cannot cross the street safely. They have to go into the road with cars, or they cannot leave their block at all.

The city agreed to fix this. They have a schedule for putting in ramps and fixing broken ones across the whole city.

If you live in or visit Philadelphia and you find:
- A street corner with NO ramp
- A ramp that's too steep, broken, or missing the bumpy yellow strip at the bottom
- A ramp in the wrong place (not where the crosswalk is)

— that may be a violation. You can report it to Liberty Resources or to the city.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

short_description: Settled class action; Philadelphia agreed to install and repair curb ramps citywide on a schedule. Compliance phase — report missing or non-compliant curb ramps.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under ADA Title II and Section 504 requiring City of Philadelphia to install and remediate curb ramps citywide on a court-supervised schedule. Compliance phase; report Title II violations to plaintiffs' counsel and city.
short_description_simple: Settled in 2019. Philadelphia must fix curb ramps citywide. If you find a corner without a ramp, with a broken ramp, or with a too-steep slope — you can report it.

case_name: Dunsmore v. San Diego County Sheriff's Department
court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California
eligibility:
The settlement protects people with disabilities who are or were incarcerated in San Diego County jail facilities.

You can report a problem if:
- You (or someone in your family) is in San Diego County Sheriff's custody.
- They have a disability — mobility, sensory, intellectual, mental health.
- They aren't getting required accommodations: physical access, interpreters, accessible programs, etc.

The settlement covers SDSD-operated facilities. Federal detention (ICE, BOP), state prisons (CDCR), and other California counties have separate obligations.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities incarcerated in SDCSO-operated facilities retain ongoing rights under the settlement. Coverage does not extend to ICE detention, federal BOP, CDCR facilities, or other California county jails.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You or your family member is in San Diego County jail.
- They have a disability.
- They are not getting accommodations they need.

This is for San Diego County jails only. Other jails have separate rules.
full_description:
This is a class-action lawsuit against the San Diego County Sheriff's Department over conditions and accessibility in San Diego County jails. Filed in March 2020 in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of California.

The ADA Title II and Section 504 claims — focused on accessibility for prisoners with mobility, sensory, intellectual, and mental health disabilities — were settled in August 2025. The settlement requires audit and remediation of physical access at all SDCSO-operated jail facilities, accessible programming, qualified ASL interpreters for deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners, reasonable modifications to disciplinary/classification/programming systems, staff training, and ongoing monitoring.

Other claims in the case — including Eighth Amendment claims about medical care, mental health treatment, and overall conditions — remain in active litigation as of mid-2026.

If you or a family member is currently in San Diego County jail and isn't getting required accommodations under the settlement, that's a settlement-violation report worth making. Contact Disability Rights California or Disability Rights Advocates.
full_description_professional:
Dunsmore, et al. v. San Diego County Sheriff's Department, 3:20-cv-00406-AJB-DDL (S.D. Cal., filed March 2, 2020), is a partially settled class action. The Title II ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504, 29 U.S.C. § 794, claims addressing jail-conditions accessibility were settled August 2025; Eighth Amendment medical-care, mental-health-treatment, and broader conditions-of-confinement claims remain in active litigation.

The ADA settlement implements: facility audit and remediation per ADA Standards and 28 C.F.R. § 35.151; programmatic accessibility under § 35.130(b)(7) for disciplinary, classification, educational, vocational, and treatment programming; qualified-interpreter provision for deaf prisoners under § 35.160; reasonable modifications; staff training; ongoing monitoring.

Compliance phase for the ADA portion ongoing. Class counsel includes Disability Rights California and Disability Rights Advocates. Current non-compliance may be reported to class counsel, DOJ Civil Rights Division under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170, California Civil Rights Department, and California Attorney General's Office.
full_description_simple:
This is a lawsuit against the San Diego County jail. Part of it is settled; part is still going.

The part about disability access — buildings, sign language interpreters, accessible programs — was settled in August 2025. The county has to:
- Fix the buildings so wheelchair users can move around.
- Provide sign language interpreters for deaf people in jail.
- Make sure people with disabilities can join programs (school, work, treatment).
- Train staff.

Other parts of the lawsuit — about medical care and mental health treatment — are still being decided.

If you or a family member is in San Diego County jail and isn't getting these accommodations, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: ADA compliance phase; remainder in active litigation

short_description: ADA accessibility claims settled August 2025; other claims active. Report jail accessibility violations.
short_description_professional: Class action against San Diego County Sheriff's Department, 3:20-cv-00406-AJB-DDL (S.D. Cal., filed March 2020). ADA Title II and Section 504 claims for jail-conditions accessibility settled August 2025; other claims (Eighth Amendment, medical care) remain in active litigation. Compliance phase for the ADA portion.
short_description_simple: ADA claims settled in August 2025. San Diego County jail was sued for failing to accommodate prisoners with disabilities. The ADA part is settled; other parts of the lawsuit are still going. If you or a family member is in San Diego County jail and isn't getting accommodations, you can report it.

case_name: Niles v. Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc.
court: U.S. District Court, Western District of Pennsylvania
eligibility:
This case is for travelers who rely on a wheelchair for mobility and who have looked into staying — or actually stayed — at a Hilton-brand hotel since 2022.

You may be part of the class if all of the following apply:
- You use a wheelchair (manual or power) as your primary means of mobility.
- You have a qualifying mobility-related disability.
- You looked at booking, attempted to book, or actually stayed in a Hilton-brand hotel during a period the lawsuit is expected to cover (approximately 2022 to the present).
- You either could not safely transfer to the bed in a designated "accessible" room, or you avoided booking because Hilton wouldn't tell you the bed height in advance.

You don't need to have been physically injured to be part of this case.
eligibility_professional:
The putative class, as pleaded, comprises all persons in the United States who: (1) possess a qualifying mobility disability within the meaning of 42 U.S.C. § 12102; (2) rely on a standard-sized wheelchair as a primary means of locomotion; and (3) during the class period have accessed Hilton's online reservation system or telephonic reservation channel seeking information regarding the accessibility of designated mobility-accessible rooms at any Hilton-branded property (including, without limitation, properties operating under the Hilton, Conrad, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hampton Inn, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, and Hilton Grand Vacations brands).

Class certification under Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(a) and (b)(2) is sought. Standing is grounded in the dignitary and equal-access injuries recognized by the ADA framework; physical injury is not a prerequisite. Class membership does not require a completed stay — a wheelchair user who was deterred from booking because Hilton failed to provide accessibility information sufficient to make an informed reservation may also fall within the class definition.
eligibility_simple:
This case is for you if all of these are true:
- You use a wheelchair to get around.
- You have a disability that affects your mobility.
- You tried to book a stay at a Hilton-brand hotel — Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Waldorf Astoria, or others.
- You either:
  - couldn't safely get into the bed in the "accessible" room, OR
  - didn't book because Hilton wouldn't tell you how high the bed was.

You don't need to have fallen or been hurt to be part of this case. The harm is being shut out, even if nothing bad physically happened to you.
full_description:
This is a federal class-action lawsuit. Christopher Niles, a wheelchair user, sued Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Hilton Management LLC, and Hilton Management Services in February 2026 in Pennsylvania federal court. The case is being heard in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania.

The lawsuit alleges that when Hilton designates a room as "accessible" or "mobility-accessible," the bed in that room is often too high or too low for a wheelchair user to safely and independently transfer to. A standard wheelchair seat sits 18-20 inches from the floor; a typical hotel mattress surface sits 25-30 inches or higher. That gap can make an otherwise accessible room unusable. The complaint also alleges that Hilton's website and call-center don't tell guests the bed height in advance, so wheelchair users can't tell whether a so-called "accessible" room will actually work for them until they've already arrived and checked in.

The lawsuit covers Hilton's full brand family — Hilton, Conrad, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hampton Inn, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, Hilton Grand Vacations, and others. The plaintiff is seeking declaratory and injunctive relief plus class certification on behalf of similarly affected travelers. The case is in its early phases.
full_description_professional:
Niles v. Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc., et al., 2:26-cv-00258 (W.D. Pa., filed Feb. 12, 2026), is a putative class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and its implementing regulations, 28 C.F.R. Part 36.

The complaint alleges that Hilton's mobility-accessible guest rooms across its brand portfolio contain bed surfaces whose vertical height renders safe, independent wheelchair-to-bed transfer impracticable for guests dependent on standard manual or power wheelchairs (seat heights of approximately 18-20 inches). Plaintiff alleges this constitutes a failure to provide an "equal opportunity" to use the lodging services provided to nondisabled guests, in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 12182(a) and the ADA Standards for Accessible Design as incorporated into 28 C.F.R. § 36.406.

The complaint further alleges that Hilton's online reservation systems and telephonic reservation channels fail to provide accurate accessibility information regarding bed dimensions sufficient to enable wheelchair users to assess whether a designated accessible room will, in fact, be accessible to them — implicating Hilton's obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e) (reservations) and the 2010 DOJ Consent Decree, United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc., 1:10-cv-01902 (D.D.C.).

Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief, certification of a nationwide class of persons with qualifying mobility disabilities who rely on standard wheelchairs and have accessed Hilton's reservation channels seeking accessibility information, attorneys' fees and costs under 42 U.S.C. § 12205, and a jury trial. The matter has been assigned to the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania.
full_description_simple:
This is a class-action lawsuit against Hilton hotels. The lawsuit was filed in February 2026 in a federal court in Pennsylvania.

A man who uses a wheelchair stayed in Hilton rooms that the hotel said were "accessible." The beds in those rooms were too high. He could not safely get from his wheelchair into the bed. A standard wheelchair seat is about 18 to 20 inches off the floor. Most hotel mattresses are 25 to 30 inches off the floor — or even higher. That makes the bed too high to use.

The lawsuit also says Hilton's website and phone reservations don't tell people how high the beds are. So a wheelchair user can't know in advance if the bed will work for them.

The lawsuit covers all Hilton brands — including Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, and Waldorf Astoria. The plaintiff is asking the court to make Hilton fix this. He is not asking for money for individual class members yet.

The case has been filed. It has not yet been certified as a class action by the judge.

key_dates.Status: Early pleadings

short_description: Hilton designates rooms as mobility-accessible without ensuring beds permit safe wheelchair transfer.
short_description_professional: Class action alleges Hilton's designated mobility-accessible rooms contain bed surfaces that prevent safe, independent wheelchair-to-bed transfer, and Hilton fails to disclose bed-height information at the time of reservation.
short_description_simple: Hilton's accessible hotel rooms often have beds that are too high to safely move into from a wheelchair.

case_name: Trivette v. Tennessee Department of Correction
court: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Tennessee
eligibility:
The settlement covers deaf and hard-of-hearing people in Tennessee state prisons (and their families/advocates).

You can report a problem if:
- The person is in Tennessee Department of Correction custody (state prisons; NOT county jails or federal BOP).
- They are deaf or hard-of-hearing.
- They aren't getting interpreters, videophones, visual alerts, or other auxiliary aids.

You don't need to have been part of the original case to report current non-compliance.
eligibility_professional: The settlement covers TDOC state-operated facilities. Tennessee county jails and federal BOP facilities are subject to separate Title II/§ 504 enforcement.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- The person is in a Tennessee state prison.
- They are deaf or hard-of-hearing.
- They aren't getting interpreters, videophones, or visual alerts.

This does not cover county jails or federal prisons in Tennessee — those have separate rules.
full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the Tennessee Department of Correction (TDOC) by deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners, settled in January 2025.

The lawsuit followed a pattern of cases against state prison systems (Virginia 2010, Kentucky 2015, Maryland 2015, Georgia 2021). The case alleged TDOC failed to provide effective communication and qualified interpretation to deaf inmates: no ASL interpreters for medical, disciplinary, classification, education, or programming; no videophones; no visual emergency alerts; deaf prisoners punished for not following orders they couldn't hear; programming exclusion that lengthened sentences.

The January 2025 settlement requires TDOC to provide qualified ASL interpreters at no charge for all major events, install videophones, provide visual and tactile emergency notification systems, train staff, implement written policies and outreach, and submit to monitoring.

The case is in long-term compliance phase. If you (or a family member) is a deaf or hard-of-hearing person in Tennessee state custody and isn't getting these accommodations, that's a settlement violation. Report it.
full_description_professional:
Trivette v. Tennessee Department of Correction (M.D. Tenn., January 2025 settlement), is a settled class action under Title II ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing TDOC's systemic failure to provide effective communication and auxiliary aids to deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates. The case follows the WLC-led template established in 2010 Virginia DOC, 2015 Kentucky (Adams), 2015 Maryland (Jarboe), and 2021 Georgia (Harris v. GDOC) settlements.

Settlement obligations: qualified ASL interpreter provision under 28 C.F.R. § 35.160 for medical, disciplinary, classification, educational, vocational, religious, and emergency interactions; videophone (TTY/VRS-equivalent) deployment; visual and tactile emergency notification; staff training; written policies; outreach mechanisms; monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Class counsel: Washington Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights and Urban Affairs (WLC) DC Prisoners' Project (likely involved given the multi-state template); confirm with TN P&A and Disability Rights TN for current counsel.

Reports of non-compliance may proceed via class counsel, DOJ § 35.170 administrative complaint, Tennessee Human Rights Commission, and the Tennessee Protection & Advocacy entity.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against Tennessee state prisons. It is now settled.

Deaf people in Tennessee prisons sued because the prisons did not help them understand what was happening — no sign language interpreters, no videophones to call family, no flashing lights for fire alarms.

In January 2025 the case was settled. Tennessee must now:
- Provide qualified sign language interpreters for free.
- Install videophones.
- Have visual and tactile emergency alerts.
- Train staff.

If you have a family member in Tennessee state prison who is deaf or hard-of-hearing and isn't getting these things — that's breaking the settlement. You can report it.

key_dates.Companion cases: VA 2010, KY 2015 (Adams), MD 2015 (Jarboe), GA 2021 (Harris v. GDOC)
key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

short_description: Settled January 2025. TN DOC required to provide qualified interpreters and accessible programs to deaf prisoners. Report violations.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under Title II ADA and Section 504 binding Tennessee Department of Correction to qualified-interpreter provision, videophone access, visual emergency notification, and accessible programming for deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners. January 2025 settlement; compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: Settled in January 2025. Tennessee prisons must provide qualified sign language interpreters and accessible programs to deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners. If your family member is a deaf inmate in Tennessee and isn't getting accommodations, you can report it.

case_name: Coen v. Georgia Department of Corrections (voluntarily dismissed)
court: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Georgia
legal_theory: ADA Title II + Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act
short_description: Deaf prisoner's pro se class-action motion against Georgia DOC over communication-access barriers. Voluntarily dismissed without prejudice Sept 18, 2018. The active class-action case on the same issue is Harris v. Georgia Department of Corrections (class certified 2021, M.D. Ga.), not Coen.

case_name: Disney Disability Access Service (DAS) Litigation (Dogali plaintiffs)
court: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Florida (Orlando Division)
defendants: Walt Disney Parks and Resorts U.S., Inc.
eligibility:
This litigation involves multiple parallel cases. You may be involved or affected if any of the following apply:
- You or a family member has a disability — developmental (autism, ADHD, intellectual disability), physical (wheelchair use, chronic illness, GI conditions), or mental (anxiety, PTSD).
- You have visited or planned to visit Walt Disney World (Florida) or Disneyland (California) within the last several years.
- You were denied DAS or assigned a "return-to-queue" or wheelchair alternative you considered inadequate for your disability.

There is no single class yet, but if the Florida Commission on Human Relations finds reasonable cause, a much broader class action could follow. If you're in Florida and want to participate, contact Andy Dogali (Dogali Law Group, Tampa). If you're in California, contact disability rights organizations tracking parallel DAS litigation. You can file a complaint with the DOJ at ADA.gov regardless of state.
eligibility_professional:
No certified class currently exists; the litigation comprises multiple individual and small-group actions. Persons potentially affected by parallel or future certification include:
- Guests with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 (developmental, physical, mental, or chronic-illness disabilities).
- Guests who attempted to use DAS at Walt Disney World (M.D. Fla.) or Disneyland (C.D. Cal.) and were either (i) denied eligibility under the October 2024 policy revision; or (ii) offered alternative accommodations (return-to-queue, wheelchair use) that did not provide equally effective access under § 12182(b)(1)(A)(ii).
- Guests subjected to the third-party "health professional" eligibility-screening process.

Class certification would require resolution of commonality and typicality questions complicated by the disability-category-specific harm theory. The Florida Commission on Human Relations investigation may yield a reasonable-cause finding that catalyzes class certification under the Florida Civil Rights Act and Fed. R. Civ. P. 23.

Parallel enforcement avenues include DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502, state-civil-rights enforcement in Florida and California, and individual Title III actions for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees.
eligibility_simple:
You may want to look into this case if:
- You or someone in your family has a disability of any kind.
- You went to Disney World or Disneyland, or were planning to.
- Disney either said you don't qualify for DAS, or gave you a different accommodation (like "leave the line and come back" or use a wheelchair) that didn't actually help.

There isn't one big class yet. But if Florida finds Disney is breaking state law, that could change.

For now:
- Florida residents: call Andy Dogali's law firm in Tampa.
- California residents: look for disability rights groups tracking DAS lawsuits.
- Anyone: file a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice at ADA.gov. It's free.
- Florida residents: also file with the Florida Commission on Human Relations — they're investigating right now.
full_description:
Walt Disney Parks operates a program called Disability Access Service (DAS) that lets qualifying guests with disabilities skip standby lines on rides at Walt Disney World in Florida and Disneyland in California. This case is a years-long, ongoing legal fight over who qualifies and what alternatives are offered to guests who don't.

The original DAS program started in 2013 in response to abuse of an older "guest assistance card" system. Under the 2013-2024 DAS, guests with documented disabilities got advance return-times for rides instead of waiting in line. In October 2024, Disney narrowed eligibility significantly — DAS is now primarily available to guests with developmental disabilities like autism. Guests with other disabilities (mobility impairments, chronic illness, GI conditions, anxiety, sensory disabilities not on the autism spectrum, etc.) are now told to use wheelchairs, "return-to-queue" alternatives, or other accommodations Disney considers equivalent.

Tampa lawyer Andy Dogali represents about 30 families with autism in Florida courts; parallel federal litigation is pending in California against Disneyland. The plaintiffs argue the eligibility-screening process improperly second-guesses who is "disabled enough," that the alternative accommodations are not actually equivalent, and that the policy disproportionately harms guests with non-developmental disabilities.

In early April 2026, mediation between a Florida plaintiff and Disney failed, and the Florida Commission on Human Relations launched a state-level investigation under the Florida Civil Rights Act. A finding of "reasonable cause" by the state could trigger fines and a green light for a much larger class action. Disney maintains the ADA does NOT require equal accommodation across different disabilities; plaintiffs disagree. The case is expected to take years.
full_description_professional:
The Disability Access Service (DAS) litigation comprises multiple parallel proceedings against Walt Disney Parks and Resorts U.S., Inc. and Walt Disney Co. challenging the DAS program under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and (in Florida) the Florida Civil Rights Act of 1992. The litigation has been ongoing since approximately 2014, with Tampa-based counsel Andy Dogali representing approximately 30 families in Florida federal court (M.D. Fla., Orlando Division, before Judge Anne Conway and subsequent panels); companion federal litigation is pending in California against Disneyland.

The October 2024 policy revision narrowed DAS eligibility to guests with "developmental disabilities like autism," replacing accommodation for many other disability categories with "return-to-queue" alternatives, mandatory wheelchair use, or other Disney-designated modifications. Plaintiffs allege three principal Title III theories: (i) the eligibility-screening process — administered in part by third-party contracted "health professionals" — constitutes an impermissible disability-status determination not authorized by the regulations; (ii) the alternative accommodations are not equally effective under the "equal opportunity" standard of 42 U.S.C. § 12182(b)(1)(A)(ii); and (iii) the policy operates with disparate impact against guests with non-developmental disabilities.

Disney's defense — articulated in its California court filings — invokes the principle that the ADA does not require identical treatment across disabilities, citing the wheelchair-priority vs. captioned-seating analogy. Plaintiffs counter that this principle does not authorize categorical exclusion of certain disability classes from a program available to others similarly situated as to the relevant access barrier (line-waiting capacity).

In April 2026, mediation between a Florida plaintiff and Disney failed; the Florida Commission on Human Relations opened a state-level investigation under the Florida Civil Rights Act. A "reasonable cause" finding could trigger administrative fines and enable a much larger class action, transforming the dispute from individualized litigation into systemic enforcement.
full_description_simple:
This is a long-running fight about who gets help at Disney World and Disneyland.

Disney has a program called DAS (Disability Access Service). It lets guests with disabilities skip the long lines on rides. People with autism use it a lot, because waiting in long crowded lines is very hard for them.

In 2024 Disney changed the rules. They said DAS is now mostly for people with autism or similar conditions. Other people with disabilities — like wheelchair users, people with chronic pain, people who get sick if they stand for a long time, people who panic in crowds — were told to use a wheelchair instead, or to "leave the line and come back," or other things Disney offered.

A lot of families said: this is unfair. The new rules don't work for our disabilities. A lawyer in Tampa named Andy Dogali is representing about 30 Florida families suing Disney. There is another lawsuit in California.

In April 2026 the state of Florida also started an investigation. Mediation failed. If Florida finds Disney is breaking state law, it could mean big fines and a much bigger lawsuit.

Disney says: the law doesn't require us to treat every disability the same way. The families and their lawyers say: yes it does. This will take years.

key_dates.Status: Ongoing; potential class certification pending state investigation outcome

legal_theory: ADA Title III + Florida Civil Rights Act
short_description: Ongoing multi-plaintiff ADA Title III litigation against Walt Disney Parks over its Disability Access Service (DAS) program. Plaintiffs allege Disney's post-2013 DAS policy and a 2024 narrowing (limiting DAS to developmental disabilities like autism) discriminate against guests with other disabilities. Florida Commission on Human Relations opened a state investigation in 2026 after mediation failed.
short_description_professional: Multi-plaintiff Title III litigation against Walt Disney Parks challenging the Disability Access Service (DAS) program, including the October 2024 policy narrowing limiting DAS eligibility to guests with developmental disabilities. Plaintiffs allege the eligibility-screening process and alternative accommodations (return-to-queue, wheelchair use) constitute disability discrimination under Title III and the Florida Civil Rights Act. Companion California federal action pending; Florida Commission on Human Relations opened state-level investigation April 2026 following failed mediation.
short_description_simple: Disney changed its disability access policy at its parks. It now mostly serves autistic guests. People with other disabilities are suing. The case has been going for years and a state investigation just started in 2026.

case_name: Fust v. First Urology, P.S.C.
court: U.S. District Court, Western District of Kentucky
eligibility:
This case is now settled. The class-action and DOJ settlement covered any patient (or potential patient) of First Urology with a mobility disability who needed help transferring from a wheelchair, scooter, or other mobility device to an exam table or imaging equipment, at all First Urology locations in Kentucky and Indiana (approximately 20 offices).

If you're experiencing this issue NOW at First Urology — staff still refusing to help you transfer, or insisting you bring your own equipment or family member — that's a settlement violation. Report it to Disability Rights Advocates at dralegal.org, file a complaint with the DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov, or file with the Kentucky Commission on Human Rights or Indiana Civil Rights Commission.
eligibility_professional:

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against First Urology, the largest urological medical practice in the greater Louisville area, with about 20 locations across Kentucky and Indiana. Three wheelchair users — Elizabeth Fust, David Allgood, and Marcus Gray — together with the Center for Accessible Living, sued in August 2020 in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Kentucky. They were represented by Disability Rights Advocates.

The lawsuit challenged First Urology's written "Lifting Policy," which said the practice would not help patients transfer from wheelchairs or other mobility devices to exam tables or imaging equipment. Patients with mobility disabilities were told they had to bring their own equipment, or bring friends, family members, or attendants to help them transfer. If a patient didn't bring their own help, First Urology refused treatment. Elizabeth Fust was refused diagnostic imaging entirely at all 20 locations and told to go to a hospital instead.

The U.S. Department of Justice opened a parallel investigation and reached a settlement: First Urology paid $60,000 and agreed to change the policy, train its staff, and provide proper transfer equipment and assistance at all its locations.

The case is now in compliance phase — the policy is gone, but advocates continue to monitor whether First Urology actually provides the equipment and assistance the settlement requires.
full_description_professional:
Fust, et al. v. First Urology, P.S.C., 3:20-cv-00562 (W.D. Ky., filed Aug. 12, 2020), is a putative class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, and the Kentucky Civil Rights Act. Plaintiffs Elizabeth Fust, David Allgood, and Marcus Gray, joined by the Center for Accessible Living, were represented by Disability Rights Advocates. The action targeted First Urology's written "Lifting Policy," which provided that patients requiring assistance to transfer from mobility devices to examination tables or diagnostic equipment must supply their own assistive devices or accompanying personnel; absent such provision, First Urology refused treatment.

A parallel DOJ enforcement action under the United States Attorney for the Western District of Kentucky resulted in a settlement requiring First Urology to: (i) pay $60,000; (ii) rescind the Lifting Policy in writing; (iii) procure accessible medical equipment (including height-adjustable exam tables and patient lifts where indicated) at its facilities; (iv) train staff on transfer assistance and ADA Title III obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(a)'s reasonable modification mandate; and (v) institute monitoring and reporting obligations.

The matter is in ongoing compliance phase. Patients encountering current refusals of transfer assistance at First Urology facilities should report violations to DRA and consider DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502. The case is significant precedent for analogous medical-practice accessibility enforcement: refusal of transfer assistance is a Title III "policy or procedure" requiring reasonable modification, not a permissible safety exception.
full_description_simple:

The U.S. Department of Justice also got involved. First Urology agreed to change the policy and pay $60,000. They had to train staff and buy the right equipment to help patients with disabilities transfer safely.

Right now the case is being monitored to make sure First Urology is actually following the new rules. If you go to a First Urology office and they refuse to help you transfer to an exam table, that is exactly the behavior the settlement banned. You can report it.

case_name: Adams v. Commonwealth of Kentucky
court: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Kentucky
eligibility:
This case is for deaf or hard-of-hearing people currently or formerly in Kentucky DOC custody (and their families/advocates) who experienced effective-communication failures.

You may benefit from this settlement if:
- You (or someone you're acting on behalf of) is deaf or hard-of-hearing.
- You are/were incarcerated in any Kentucky DOC facility (state prisons; this does NOT cover county jails, which are separate).
- You needed interpretation, a videophone, a visual emergency alert, or other auxiliary aids — and did not get them, or had to pay for them, or had family members improperly used as interpreters.

You don't have to have been part of the original 2014 class to report current violations.
eligibility_professional:
The settled class encompasses deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals in Kentucky DOC custody from the settlement-applicable period through final resolution. The settlement creates ongoing obligations binding on KDOC, enforceable by both class members and the settlement-administrator entities.

The settlement does NOT cover Kentucky county or municipal jail facilities, which are independent governmental entities under Title II and require separate enforcement. Parallel Title II claims against county jails should proceed through DOJ administrative complaint or independent litigation.

Class members and current Kentucky DOC inmates may report current non-compliance through:
- Direct notice to class counsel (Washington Lawyers' Committee, dcprisonersproject@washlaw.org).
- DOJ Disability Rights Section administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
- Kentucky Commission on Human Rights administrative complaint under the Kentucky Civil Rights Act.
- Direct settlement-enforcement motion in E.D. Ky. for substantive non-compliance affecting the named plaintiffs or class members.
eligibility_simple:
This case is for deaf or hard-of-hearing people in Kentucky prisons (and their families).

You can report a problem if:
- You or someone in your family is deaf or hard-of-hearing.
- You are or were in a Kentucky state prison.
- You needed an interpreter, a videophone to call family, or a visual fire alarm — and did not get one. Or you had to use a family member as your interpreter (which is not allowed).

You don't have to have been part of the original case from 2014. The rules cover all deaf people in Kentucky prisons now.

This case does NOT cover county jails. If your family member is in a county jail, the ADA still protects them but the rules are different — Ada can help you figure out the next step.
full_description:
This was a federal class-action lawsuit against the Commonwealth of Kentucky, the Kentucky Department of Corrections, and the Kentucky Justice and Public Safety Cabinet. Two deaf prisoners — Oscar Adams and Michael Knights — sued in January 2014 in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Kentucky.

They alleged that Kentucky's prison system systematically failed to provide effective communication to deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates: no qualified sign-language interpreters for medical visits, disciplinary hearings, classification, education, religious services, or work assignments; no videophones for outside communication; no visual or tactile emergency alerts; no training for prison staff. Deaf inmates were being punished for not responding to verbal commands they couldn't hear, missing classes they couldn't access, and serving longer sentences as a result.

The case was settled in May 2015. Kentucky agreed to provide qualified interpreters at no charge for all major events, install videophones, give visual emergency notifications, train staff on disability rights, and submit to monitoring. The named plaintiffs received $1,500 each in damages; their attorneys received $250,000 in fees and costs.

The case is now in long-term compliance phase. If you or someone you know is a deaf or hard-of-hearing prisoner in Kentucky custody and is NOT getting interpreters, videophones, visual alerts, or other auxiliary aids — that's a violation of the settlement. Report it.
full_description_professional:
Adams v. Commonwealth of Kentucky, 3:14-cv-00001 (E.D. Ky., filed Jan. 1, 2014), is a settled class action under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794. Plaintiffs Oscar Adams and Michael Knights, both deaf inmates within Kentucky DOC custody, challenged the Commonwealth's systemic failure to provide effective communication, qualified ASL interpretation, auxiliary aids and services, and accessible programming.

The May 2015 settlement, modeled on the 2010 WLC settlement with Virginia DOC and contemporaneous with the Jarboe v. Maryland DPSCS settlement, requires KDOC to: (i) provide qualified ASL interpreters at no cost to inmates for all medical, disciplinary, classification, educational, vocational, and religious encounters; (ii) install videophones (TTY/VRS-equivalent) for outside communication; (iii) provide visual and tactile emergency notification systems; (iv) train custodial and program staff on disability-rights obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 35.160; (v) implement written policies, periodic outreach to deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates, and ongoing monitoring. Named-plaintiff individual damages were $1,500 each; attorneys' fees and costs of $250,000.

The matter remains in compliance phase. Last docketed activity Dec. 17, 2020. Plaintiffs were represented by Washington Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights and Urban Affairs (WLC), Weil Gotshal & Manges LLP, and Belzley Bathurst Attorneys.

Current Kentucky inmates (or family members or advocates of inmates) experiencing non-compliance should report under multiple parallel mechanisms: direct settlement-enforcement notice to WLC, DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170, and Kentucky Commission on Human Rights administrative complaint. The settlement creates an ongoing private right to enforce compliance.
full_description_simple:
This case was a lawsuit against Kentucky prisons. It is now settled.

Two deaf men in Kentucky prisons sued in 2014. They said the prisons did not help them understand what was happening — there were no sign language interpreters for medical visits, no interpreters for hearings, no videophones to call family, no flashing lights to warn them about fires or emergencies. They were being punished for not following orders they could not hear.

In 2015 the case was settled. Kentucky now has to:
- Provide qualified sign language interpreters for free.
- Install videophones so deaf inmates can call family using sign language.
- Have visual or tactile fire alarms and emergency alerts.
- Train staff on how to work with deaf people.
- Let outside groups monitor whether they are actually doing these things.

If you know a deaf or hard-of-hearing person in a Kentucky prison RIGHT NOW who is not getting these things — that's breaking the settlement. You can report it.

key_dates.Status: Long-term compliance monitoring

short_description: Settled May 2015. Kentucky DOC required to provide qualified ASL interpreters, videophones, visual emergency alerts, and accessible programs to deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under Title II ADA and Section 504; Kentucky DOC bound to provide qualified ASL interpreters, videophone access, visual emergency notification, auxiliary aids and services, and accessible programming for deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates. case_name: National Federation of the Blind of California v. Uber Technologies, Inc.
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
eligibility:
This settlement protects blind and visually disabled people nationwide who use service animals and use (or want to use) Uber.

You can report a current Uber service-animal discrimination incident if:
- You're blind or visually disabled.
- You use a guide dog or other trained service animal.
- An Uber driver refused to pick you up because of your service animal, drove away when seeing your dog, asked you to "leave the dog behind," charged you an improper "cleaning fee" because of your dog, or otherwise treated you differently because of the service animal.

You don't have to be a class member from the original 2014-2016 case. Note: this case is about Uber specifically. Lyft has its own separate cases and obligations.
eligibility_professional:
The settlement creates ongoing nationwide protections for legally blind and visually disabled persons using service animals who use or seek to use the Uber rider application. Standing under Title III is established by access denial; physical injury is not required. California Class members additionally possess standing for statutory damages under Cal. Civ. Code § 52(a).

Coverage is limited to Uber Technologies, Inc. and its subsidiary/affiliate entities (UberX, Rasier-LLC, Rasier-CA LLC, Uber USA LLC, etc.). Other rideshare entities (Lyft) and traditional taxi services are covered by Title III and may be subject to parallel litigation, but are not bound by this settlement's specific enforcement mechanisms.

Reports of post-settlement violations should be made to: (i) the NFB testing program; (ii) class counsel (DRA at dralegal.org); (iii) DOJ Civil Rights Division under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502; (iv) Uber's in-app complaint system (which is itself a settlement-required mechanism); (v) state civil-rights agencies in jurisdictions with applicable laws (CA Unruh Act, NY Human Rights Law, etc.).
eligibility_simple:
The settlement protects blind people in the United States who use service animals and use Uber.

You can report a problem today if:
- You're blind or have low vision.
- You use a service animal (like a guide dog).
- An Uber driver wouldn't pick you up because of your dog.
- Or a driver drove away when they saw your dog.
- Or you got charged an extra fee because of your dog.

You don't have to have been part of the original case. The rules apply to all blind Uber riders today.

This is about UBER. Lyft, taxis, and other rideshare services have their own separate rules.
full_description:
This was a federal class-action lawsuit against Uber Technologies, Inc. and its UberX subsidiary by the National Federation of the Blind (NFB), its California affiliate, and three individual blind riders — Michael Hingson, Michael Kelly, and Michael Pedersen. They sued in September 2014 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The case is settled and now in compliance phase.

Plaintiffs alleged that UberX drivers were routinely refusing to transport blind riders accompanied by guide dogs or other service animals, and that Uber wasn't taking adequate steps to stop the discrimination. Drivers cited allergies, fear of dogs, religious objections, or just refused without explanation.

The case settled in December 2016 with comprehensive injunctive relief: Uber agreed that drivers must transport service-animal users; knowing refusals get the driver permanently removed; allergy or fear of dogs is not a valid excuse; Uber must track every complaint and report aggregated data to class counsel; NFB runs a testing program; a third-party monitor oversees compliance.

If an Uber driver refuses to take you because of your service animal today, you should still report it. Plaintiffs are represented by Disability Rights Advocates, TRE Legal, and Rosen Bien Galvan & Grunfeld.
full_description_professional:
National Federation of the Blind of California, et al. v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 3:14-cv-04086 (N.D. Cal., filed Sept. 9, 2014), was a class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq.; the California Unruh Civil Rights Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§ 51 & 52; and the California Disabled Persons Act, Cal. Civ. Code § 54. Plaintiffs NFB, NFB-CA, and three individual blind riders (Michael Hingson, Michael Kelly, Michael Pedersen) alleged Uber's on-demand transportation network discriminated against blind riders with service animals through driver-level refusals, allergic/phobic exclusion, and discriminatory cleaning-fee assessments.

The DOJ filed a Statement of Interest on Dec. 23, 2014 supporting plaintiffs, citing DOT regulations at 49 C.F.R. § 37.3 and pt. 37, app. D § 37.23 (Title III applies to "demand-responsive" services without fixed routes; private entities cannot contract away ADA obligations; entities must permit service animals to accompany owners into vehicles).

The Dec. 6, 2016 settlement (judgment entered Dec. 15, 2016) provides comprehensive injunctive relief: (i) driver-platform acknowledgments via pop-up interactive questionnaire and contract amendments; (ii) quarterly email reminders to drivers; (iii) permanent platform removal for knowing service-animal refusals; (iv) permanent platform removal for repeated plausible complaints regardless of intent; (v) prohibition of allergy or fear-of-dogs defenses; (vi) cleaning-fee policy reform; (vii) complaint-tracking and aggregated reporting to class counsel; (viii) NFB-administered testing program with blind riders; (ix) retired Judge Margaret A. Nagle appointed as neutral monitor with authority to extend the agreement by 1.5 years for substantial non-compliance.

Monetary terms: $2.38M attorneys' fees and costs; $15,000 individual damages each named plaintiff; $75,000/yr to NFB during settlement term, with provision for fourth-year payment if extended. As of July 2020 the court retained ongoing jurisdiction; the settlement enforcement mechanism remains active.

Plaintiffs represented by Disability Rights Advocates (DRA), TRE Legal Practice, and Rosen Bien Galvan & Grunfeld LLP.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against Uber. It is now settled. The settlement is still in effect — it tells Uber what to do.

Three blind men who use guide dogs sued Uber in 2014. They said Uber drivers kept refusing to give them rides because of their dogs. Drivers said things like "I'm allergic" or "I don't like dogs." Sometimes they drove away when they saw the dog. This is against the ADA — businesses have to serve people with service animals, even if the driver doesn't like dogs.

In 2016 the case was settled. Now Uber has to:
- Tell every driver: you must give rides to people with service animals.
- Permanently remove drivers who knowingly refuse.
- Permanently remove drivers who get more than one complaint, even if they say they didn't mean it.
- Tell drivers that allergies and fear of dogs are NOT a good reason.
- Track every complaint and tell the lawyers about them.
- Let the National Federation of the Blind send blind test riders to check on drivers.
- Reform their cleaning fee rules so people don't get charged extra because of a service animal.

If a driver refuses you today, you can:
- Report it to Uber inside the app.
- Tell the National Federation of the Blind testing program.
- Call Disability Rights Advocates.
- File a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase; ongoing monitoring

legal_theory: ADA Title III + Unruh Civil Rights Act

eligibility_simple:
You can report a problem if:
- You use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or other mobility device.
- The broken sidewalk or missing ramp is in the city of LA.
- The problem stopped you from getting where you needed to go.

NOT covered:
- LA County roads outside the city.
- LA Metro buses or trains.
- Other cities like Beverly Hills or Santa Monica.
full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of Los Angeles, filed by Willits, et al., a group of LA residents with mobility disabilities, in 2010 in the U.S. District Court for the Central District of California (2:10-cv-05782).

The lawsuit alleged that LA's sidewalk infrastructure had decades of deferred maintenance — cracked, lifted, broken, and missing sections, plus missing or non-compliant curb ramps — that made enormous portions of the public pedestrian network unusable for wheelchair users, scooter users, and others with mobility disabilities.

In 2015, the case settled with a landmark $1.4 BILLION commitment from LA — the largest sidewalk-accessibility settlement in U.S. history. The city agreed to spend the money over 30 years, with priority routes, milestones, and oversight.

The case is in long-term compliance phase. Reports of inaccessible sidewalks, broken curb ramps, or improperly maintained pedestrian rights-of-way can be filed with the city or with plaintiffs' counsel.
full_description_professional:
Willits, et al. v. City of Los Angeles, 2:10-cv-05782 (C.D. Cal., filed 2010), is a settled class action under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., resolving systemic claims regarding the City's pedestrian-rights-of-way accessibility deficiencies.

The 2015 settlement requires the City to expend $1.4 billion over 30 years on sidewalk and curb-ramp remediation, with priority routing, milestone reporting, and ongoing court supervision. The settlement is widely cited as the largest municipal-accessibility settlement in U.S. history and is precedent for parallel actions against other major cities (Seattle, NYC, Philadelphia).

The matter remains in compliance phase. Reporting non-compliance can be made via the city's 311 system, to plaintiffs' counsel, or via DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against the City of Los Angeles. It is now settled.

A group of LA residents who use wheelchairs sued the city in 2010. They said LA's sidewalks were so broken that wheelchair users could not use them. Many corners had no ramps at all.

In 2015 the city agreed to settle. LA must spend $1.4 BILLION dollars — over 30 years — fixing sidewalks and putting in curb ramps.

If you live in or visit LA and you find:
- A sidewalk that's too broken to roll on
- A street corner with no ramp
- A ramp that's too steep or broken

— you can report it. The city is supposed to be fixing these.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, multi-decade implementation

short_description: Settled 2015, $1.4B commitment. LA on 30-year remediation schedule for sidewalks and curb cuts. Report violations.
short_description_professional: Settled class action requiring City of Los Angeles to expend $1.4 billion over 30 years remediating sidewalks and curb ramps for ADA Title II accessibility. Compliance phase; ongoing reporting via plaintiffs' counsel and city.
short_description_simple: Settled in 2015. LA agreed to spend $1.4 billion fixing sidewalks and curb ramps over 30 years. If you find a broken sidewalk or missing ramp in LA, report it.

case_name: Alcazar v. Fashion Nova, Inc.
court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California
eligibility:
You may be a class member if all of the following apply:
- You're legally blind (or have a vision disability requiring screen-reader software for web access).
- You attempted to access Fashion Nova's website (www.fashionnova.com) using screen-reader software at any point from February 26, 2018 to the present.

There are two classes:
- A Nationwide Class — includes anyone in the US who fits the description above. The Nationwide Class is eligible for the injunctive relief (Fashion Nova fixing the website), but no individual cash payment.
- A California Class — includes Nationwide Class members who were in California when they tried to access the website. California Class members may receive up to $4,000 each under California's Unruh Civil Rights Act.

The claim filing deadline was October 20, 2025, so new cash-claim filings are closed. You can still be part of the Nationwide Class for purposes of injunctive relief if the settlement is approved, even without a claim filed. The case is on hold pending the court's approval decision.

If you tried to file a claim but couldn't because the claims website was inaccessible to you, that's exactly the issue the DOJ raised — write to Disability Rights Advocates or another disability-rights legal organization to ask about your options.
eligibility_professional:
The certified Classes (Sept. 6, 2022) are:

(1) Nationwide Class (Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(b)(2), injunctive relief): All legally blind individuals who have attempted to access Fashion Nova's Website by the use of screen-reading software during the applicable limitations period (Feb. 26, 2018) up to and including final judgment.

(2) California Class (Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(b)(3), statutory damages under Unruh Act): All legally blind individuals in the State of California who have attempted to access Fashion Nova's Website by the use of screen-reading software during the applicable limitations period up to and including final judgment.

Standing under Title III is established by the dignitary and equal-access injuries recognized in the public-accommodations context; California Class members additionally possess standing for statutory damages under Cal. Civ. Code § 52(a) at $4,000 per offense, subject to the proposed settlement's $4,000 cap per class member. Class membership does not require completed purchase or attempted purchase; access attempt with screen-reader software is sufficient.

The claims-filing deadline expired October 20, 2025. Class members who failed to perfect claims due to the accessibility barriers in the settlement-administrator's claims website (the subject of DOJ's Feb. 2, 2026 Statement of Interest) should consult class counsel or independent disability-rights counsel; remedial measures may be available pending the Court's resolution of the DOJ-flagged claims-process concerns.
eligibility_simple:
You may be in this class if:
- You're legally blind, or you use a screen reader to use websites.
- You tried to use Fashion Nova's website (www.fashionnova.com) any time from February 2018 to now.

There are two parts of the class:
- A nationwide group — anyone in the US who fits.
- A California group — anyone in the nationwide group who lives or was in California when they tried to use the site. The California group is the only group that can get money. Up to $4,000 each.

The deadline to file a claim was October 20, 2025. New claims aren't being accepted right now.

If you missed the deadline because the claims website didn't work with your screen reader, that's exactly what the U.S. Department of Justice complained about. Reach out to a disability rights lawyer or call Disability Rights Advocates to ask what to do.
full_description:
This is a federal class-action lawsuit against Fashion Nova, an online clothing retailer. The plaintiff, Juan Alcazar, is legally blind and uses screen-reader software to navigate websites. He sued in 2020 because Fashion Nova's website wasn't compatible with his screen reader — he couldn't shop, browse, or complete a purchase without barriers a sighted person doesn't face. The case is being heard in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California.

In September 2022, the court certified two classes: a nationwide class of all legally blind people who tried to access Fashion Nova's website with a screen reader, and a California subclass eligible for monetary damages under California's Unruh Civil Rights Act.

In June 2025, Fashion Nova and the plaintiffs agreed to a proposed settlement: $5.15 million total, up to $4,000 per eligible California class member, and a promise from Fashion Nova to bring its website into substantial conformance with WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards within 180 days. Any leftover money would go to the American Foundation for the Blind.

But the settlement has not been finalized. On February 2, 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a Statement of Interest opposing the settlement. The DOJ argued that the proposed injunctive relief is too vague to actually fix the website, that it has no mechanism for monitoring or enforcement, and — strikingly — that the plaintiffs' own settlement-claims website is itself inaccessible to blind users. The court held a final-approval hearing on February 12, 2026, and set an additional evidentiary hearing for March 30, 2026 to investigate the DOJ's concerns about the claims website. As of mid-2026, the court has not yet ruled.

The deadline to file a claim was October 20, 2025. The case is unusual because most website-accessibility lawsuits settle quietly and individually; this one became a class action AND drew DOJ scrutiny. The outcome will affect how courts approach website-accessibility class settlements going forward.
full_description_professional:
Alcazar v. Fashion Nova, Inc., 4:20-cv-01434-JST (N.D. Cal., filed Feb. 26, 2020), is a class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and the California Unruh Civil Rights Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§ 51 & 52. Plaintiff Juan Alcazar alleges Fashion Nova's e-commerce website fails to comply with the ADA Standards for Accessible Design as applied to public-accommodation websites, denying screen-reader users full and equal access to the goods and services offered to sighted consumers.

On September 6, 2022, Judge Jon S. Tigar certified two classes pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(b)(2) and (b)(3): a Nationwide Class for injunctive relief and a California Class for statutory damages under the Unruh Act. The Court denied an initial motion for preliminary approval of a proposed settlement on December 20, 2024 (Dkt. 198). The parties revised the proposed agreement and reached a settlement of $5.15 million in June 2025, with terms including (i) up to $4,000 per eligible California class member; (ii) injunctive relief requiring Fashion Nova to achieve substantial conformance with WCAG 2.1 within 180 days; (iii) approximately $2.52 million in attorneys' fees and costs; (iv) residual funds to the American Foundation for the Blind. The claims deadline was October 20, 2025.

On February 2, 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a Statement of Interest opposing final approval. The DOJ argued (i) the proposed injunctive relief constitutes only a generic recitation of statutory obligations without enforceable accessibility standards or monitoring mechanisms; (ii) the disproportion between attorneys' fees and class-member benefits warrants scrutiny; and (iii) the settlement administrator's claims website itself contains accessibility barriers identified by a DOJ-retained expert, potentially impairing class members' ability to perfect their claims. The Court held its final-approval hearing on February 12, 2026, and set a further evidentiary hearing for March 30, 2026 to address the DOJ's claims-website allegations. Final approval remains pending. The matter is significant as one of the largest publicly known website-accessibility class settlements and is expected to inform DOJ scrutiny of future Title III class-action settlements involving public-accommodation websites.

Plaintiffs are represented by Thiago M. Coelho (Wilshire Law Firm PLC); Fashion Nova is represented by Amy P. Lally (Sidley Austin LLP).
full_description_simple:
This is a class-action lawsuit against Fashion Nova, an online clothing store. It was filed in 2020 in a federal court in California.

The plaintiff is a man named Juan Alcazar. He's blind, and he uses screen-reader software to use websites. Screen readers turn what's on a screen into speech or braille so blind people can use a computer or phone. He sued Fashion Nova because their shopping website didn't work with screen readers. He couldn't shop on it the way sighted people could.

In 2022, the court agreed to let the case move forward as a class action — meaning, on behalf of all blind people who had tried to use the website.

In 2025, the two sides reached a settlement: Fashion Nova would pay $5.15 million and fix the website. Up to $4,000 of that money could go to each blind shopper in California who filed a claim.

But the judge has not approved the settlement yet. In February 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice stepped in and said the settlement was a bad deal — that it wouldn't actually fix the website, and that the lawyers running the claims process had set up a website that was itself not accessible to blind people. The judge held hearings in February and March 2026 to look into this. As of now, no final decision.

The deadline to file a claim was October 20, 2025.

key_dates.Status: Final approval pending; outcome of evidentiary hearing not yet on record

questions.purpose: Class definition requires Harris County registration.

questions.purpose: Class definition requires qualifying print disability.
